title: panproto: Developer Guide
text:
Preface
This is the panproto Developer Guide — an architecture reference and contributor handbook for the panproto project. It documents the internals of the universal schema migration engine: how crates are organized, how the algebraic machinery works under the hood, and how to contribute effectively.

title: panproto: Developer Guide
text:
Who this guide is for
This guide is written for three audiences:

Contributors submitting bug fixes, performance improvements, or new features to the panproto codebase.
Protocol authors adding support for new schema languages (e.g., a new IDL or data-definition format) by implementing panproto’s protocol trait.
Researchers who want to understand panproto’s internals — its use of Generalized Algebraic Theories, W-type instances, and bidirectional lenses — in order to extend or build on the engine.

If you are a user of panproto who wants to learn how to define schemas, run migrations, and use the TypeScript SDK, start with the panproto Tutorial instead. The tutorial teaches you how to use panproto; this guide explains how it works.

title: panproto: Developer Guide
text:
Prerequisites
You should be comfortable with:

Rust (ownership, traits, generics, Result-based error handling). The codebase targets Edition 2024.
Basic TypeScript, enough to read the SDK wrapper and its tests.
Algebraic concepts like sorts, operations, and equations. You do not need a category theory background — the tutorial’s category theory cheatsheet provides a gentle introduction — but familiarity with algebraic thinking will help.

title: panproto: Developer Guide
text:
How to navigate this guide
The guide is organized into six parts:
Part I — Getting Started. Read 1  Welcome to panproto Development and 2  Your First Contribution to understand the project’s goals, clone the repository, and land your first pull request. This is the recommended starting point for all new contributors.
Part II — Development Environment. Chapters 3–4 cover building, testing, debugging, and profiling. Return here whenever you need to set up a new machine or diagnose a test failure.
Part III — Architecture & Internals. Chapters 5–15 are deep dives into each crate and subsystem. Read the architecture overview first, then jump to whichever crate you are working on.
Part IV — Contributing Process. Chapters 16–18 cover coding conventions, the testing philosophy, and the PR workflow. Read these before opening your first pull request.
Part V — Project Operations. Chapter 19 covers the release process and versioning policy.
Appendices. The glossary (Appendix A) defines every domain term. The source code map (Appendix B) is a directory-level index of the entire repository. The troubleshooting FAQ (Appendix C) addresses common build and test issues.






TipQuick-start path



If you want to land a contribution as fast as possible: read chapters 1 and 2, build the project (chapter 3), make your change, then read chapters 16–18 before opening your PR.

Conventions used in this guide

Inline code like panproto-gat refers to a crate name.
File paths like crates/panproto-gat/src/theory.rs are relative to the repository root.
Shell commands assume a Unix-like environment (macOS or Linux).
Callout boxes highlight important notes, tips, and warnings.


Aaron Steven White — March 2026

title: 1  Welcome to panproto Development
text:
1.1 What is panproto?
panproto is a universal schema migration engine. It treats every schema language — SQL DDL, Protobuf, GraphQL, AT Protocol Lexicon, JSON Schema, and any future format — as a presentation of a Generalized Algebraic Theory (GAT). A protocol in panproto is parameterized by two GATs: one that describes the structural sorts and operations of the schema language, and one that describes its constraint system. By computing colimits (pushouts) of theory morphisms, panproto can translate schemas across languages, compile migrations between schema versions, and verify round-trip correctness through bidirectional lenses — all from a single algebraic core.

title: 1  Welcome to panproto Development
text:
1.2 Who this guide is for
This developer guide serves three audiences:
Contributors. You want to fix a bug, improve performance, or add a feature. You need to understand the crate structure, the build system, and the contribution workflow. Start with this chapter and Chapter 2, then jump to the architecture chapters relevant to your change.
Protocol authors. You want to add support for a new schema language by implementing the Protocol trait in panproto-protocols. You need to understand how GATs map to schema constructs and how the protocol validation pipeline works. After onboarding, read chapters 7 and 12.
Researchers. You are interested in panproto’s theoretical foundations — W-type instances, asymmetric lenses, migration compilation via colimits — and want to understand how the mathematics is implemented. After onboarding, start with chapters 5–6 and work through the architecture section sequentially.

section: 1.5 The crate landscape at a glance
text:
1.5 The crate landscape at a glance
panproto is a Cargo workspace with 10 crates, a TypeScript SDK, an integration test suite, and CI/CD workflows:







Crate
Role




panproto-gat
GAT engine: sorts, operations, equations, morphisms, colimits, models


panproto-schema
Schema representation: vertices, edges, hyper-edges, constraints


panproto-inst
W-type and functor instances: the data layer


panproto-mig
Migration engine: compile, restrict, lift, compose, invert


panproto-lens
Bidirectional lenses: symmetric, asymmetric, combinators, law checking


panproto-check
Breaking-change detection: diff, classify, report


panproto-protocols
Built-in protocols: AT Protocol, Protobuf, GraphQL, JSON Schema, SQL


panproto-core
Re-exports and shared types


panproto-wasm
WASM boundary: slab allocator, MessagePack serialization, JS API


panproto-cli
CLI binary



Chapter 5 covers how these crates depend on each other and how data flows through the system.

Before you begin, install:

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.2 Step 1: Fork and clone
Fork https://github.com/panproto/panproto on GitHub, then clone your fork:
git clone git@github.com:YOUR_USERNAME/panproto.git
cd panproto

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.3 Step 2: Understand the workspace
The repository is a Cargo workspace. Here is the workspace definition from the root Cargo.toml:
[workspace]
resolver = "3"
members = [
    "crates/panproto-gat",
    "crates/panproto-schema",
    "crates/panproto-inst",
    "crates/panproto-mig",
    "crates/panproto-lens",
    "crates/panproto-check",
    "crates/panproto-protocols",
    "crates/panproto-core",
    "crates/panproto-wasm",
    "crates/panproto-cli",
    "crates/panproto-io",
    "crates/panproto-vcs",
    "tests/integration",
]

[workspace.package]
version = "0.2.0"
edition = "2024"
rust-version = "1.85"
license = "MIT"
repository = "https://github.com/panproto/panproto"

Every crates/panproto-* directory is an independent crate. The tests/integration crate holds cross-crate integration tests. The TypeScript SDK lives in sdk/typescript/.

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.4 Step 3: Build the workspace
cargo build --workspace
This compiles all 10 crates and the integration test crate. The first build downloads dependencies and takes a few minutes; incremental builds are fast.






Note



The workspace uses resolver = "3" (the Rust 2024 edition resolver). Make sure your Rust toolchain is 1.85 or newer: rustup update stable.

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.5 Step 4: Run the test suite
cargo nextest run --workspace
All tests should pass on a clean checkout. If any fail, check Chapter 25 for common issues and fixes.






TipWhy nextest?



panproto uses cargo-nextest instead of cargo test for test isolation — each test runs in its own process, preventing shared mutable state from causing flaky results. See Chapter 3 for details.

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.6 Step 5: Build the WASM target
wasm-pack build crates/panproto-wasm --target web
This compiles panproto-wasm to WebAssembly and generates JavaScript bindings in crates/panproto-wasm/pkg/. If this fails with a missing target error, run:
rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.7 Step 6: Build the TypeScript SDK
cd sdk/typescript
pnpm install
pnpm build
This compiles the TypeScript sources and runs type checking. The SDK wraps the WASM module and provides the high-level Panproto class.

title: 2  Your First Contribution
2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.9 Step 8: Verify the full suite
Before committing, run the complete test suite to make sure nothing else broke:
cargo nextest run --workspace






ImportantAlways run the full suite



Changes to protocol definitions can affect schema validation, migration compilation, and integration tests. A passing per-crate test does not guarantee the full suite passes.

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.10 Step 9: Commit and push
panproto uses Conventional Commits:
git checkout -b feat/atproto-min-length
git add crates/panproto-protocols/src/atproto.rs
git commit -m "feat(protocols): add minLength constraint sort to AT Protocol"
git push origin feat/atproto-min-length
Common prefixes:



Prefix
Use for




feat
New feature or capability


fix
Bug fix


refactor
Code restructuring with no behavior change


test
Adding or updating tests


docs
Documentation changes


perf
Performance improvement


chore
Build, CI, dependency updates

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.11 Step 10: Open a pull request
Go to your fork on GitHub and open a pull request against main. The PR template will guide you through:

Description — what the change does and why.
Testing — how you verified it works.
Breaking changes — whether any public API changed.

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.12 What happens next
After you push, CI runs automatically:

Lint — cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings with pedantic and nursery lints.
Test — cargo nextest run --workspace on Linux, macOS, and Windows.
WASM — wasm-pack build and wasm-pack test --headless --chrome.
TypeScript — pnpm build && pnpm test in sdk/typescript/.
Format — cargo fmt --check and prettier --check for TypeScript.







Note



CI must pass before a maintainer reviews your PR. If a CI job fails, check the logs — Chapter 25 covers the most common failure modes and how to reproduce them locally.

text:
2.13 The contribution lifecycle






flowchart LR
    A["Fork"] --> B["Clone"]
    B --> C["Build<br>cargo build --workspace"]
    C --> D["Test<br>cargo nextest run --workspace"]
    D --> E["Make change"]

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.14 Checklist
Before opening your PR, verify:

cargo build --workspace succeeds
cargo nextest run --workspace passes
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings is clean
cargo fmt --check passes
wasm-pack build crates/panproto-wasm --target web succeeds
cd sdk/typescript && pnpm build && pnpm test passes
Commit message follows conventional format
PR description explains the what and why

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.15 Types of contributions
Not sure what to work on? Here are some common contribution types:
Good first issues. Look for issues labeled good first issue on the issue tracker. These are scoped, well-described tasks suitable for newcomers.
Bug fixes. Reproduce the bug with a test, fix the code, verify the test passes. Use fix(crate): description as the commit prefix.
New protocol support. Adding a new schema language means implementing the Protocol trait in panproto-protocols. See chapters 7 and 12 for the details of what a protocol requires.
Performance improvements. Run the benchmarks (chapter 4), identify a bottleneck, optimize, and verify with before/after benchmark numbers. Use perf(crate): description as the commit prefix.
Documentation. Improvements to rustdoc, this developer guide, or the tutorial are always welcome. Use docs: description as the commit prefix.

title: 2  Your First Contribution
text:
2.16 Summary
You now know how to:

Set up the development environment
Build and test the entire workspace
Make a change to a protocol definition
Commit with conventional format and open a PR
Understand what CI checks run

The next two chapters cover the build system and debugging tools in more depth. When you are ready to understand the code you are changing, jump to the architecture chapters (Part III).
For the complete coding conventions and PR expectations, see chapters 16–18.

3.1 Toolchain requirements
This chapter is the complete reference for building panproto, running tests, and understanding the test matrix. Return here whenever you set up a new machine or need to debug a build issue.
Install the Rust target and tools:

title: 3  Building and Testing
text:
3.2 Building the Rust workspace

3.2.1 Full workspace build
cargo build --workspace
This compiles all 10 crates and the integration test crate in debug mode. Incremental builds after the first compile are fast — typically under 5 seconds for single-crate changes.


3.2.2 Single crate
cargo build -p panproto-gat
Useful when iterating on one crate. Downstream crates are not recompiled.


3.2.3 Release build
The workspace defines two release profiles:
[profile.release]
lto = true
codegen-units = 1
opt-level = 3
strip = true

[profile.release-wasm]
inherits = "release"
opt-level = "z"
release enables LTO, single codegen unit, and symbol stripping for maximum performance. release-wasm inherits from release but uses opt-level = "z" to minimize binary size for the WASM target.
# Native release build
cargo build --workspace --release

# WASM release build (used by CI for npm publishing)
cargo build -p panproto-wasm --profile release-wasm --target wasm32-unknown-unknown

title: 3  Building and Testing
text:
3.3 Building the WASM target

3.3.1 For local testing
wasm-pack build crates/panproto-wasm --target web
This produces crates/panproto-wasm/pkg/ with .wasm, .js, and .d.ts files. The --target web flag generates ES module imports suitable for browsers and test runners.


3.3.2 For npm publishing
wasm-pack build crates/panproto-wasm --target bundler --release
The --target bundler flag generates output compatible with bundlers like webpack and Vite. CI uses this for the @panproto/core npm package.






ImportantMissing target error



If wasm-pack build fails with error[E0463]: can't find crate for 'core', you are missing the WASM target. Run rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown.

title: 3  Building and Testing
text:
3.4 Building the TypeScript SDK
cd sdk/typescript
pnpm install
pnpm build
pnpm build runs the TypeScript compiler and produces JavaScript output in dist/. The SDK imports the WASM module generated by wasm-pack, so you must build the WASM target first.






TipRebuild pipeline



When making changes that span Rust and TypeScript, rebuild in order:

cargo build -p panproto-wasm
wasm-pack build crates/panproto-wasm --target web
cd sdk/typescript && pnpm build

title: 3  Building and Testing
text:
3.5 Test matrix
panproto has five categories of tests. CI runs all of them on every pull request.

3.5.1 Unit tests (per crate)
Each crate has unit tests in #[cfg(test)] modules within its source files. Run a single crate’s tests:
cargo nextest run -p panproto-gat
cargo nextest run -p panproto-schema
cargo nextest run -p panproto-inst
cargo nextest run -p panproto-mig
cargo nextest run -p panproto-lens
cargo nextest run -p panproto-check
cargo nextest run -p panproto-protocols
cargo nextest run -p panproto-wasm


3.5.2 Full Rust test suite
cargo nextest run --workspace
This runs every Rust test across all crates, including the integration test crate. On a modern machine this takes 30–60 seconds.


3.5.3 Integration tests
The tests/integration crate contains cross-crate tests that exercise full pipelines:
cargo nextest run -p panproto-integration
These tests cover scenarios like:

AT Protocol recursive schema round-trips
Cross-protocol migration (e.g., Protobuf to SQL)
Breaking-change detection on real schema diffs
WASM boundary serialization fidelity
Lens law verification on composed migrations



3.5.4 Property tests
Several crates use proptest for property-based testing. These run as part of the normal unit test suite — no special invocation is needed. To run more cases:
PROPTEST_CASES=10000 cargo nextest run -p panproto-gat
To reproduce a failure from a seed:
PROPTEST_SEED=12345678 cargo nextest run -p panproto-gat -- test_name


3.5.5 Running a single test
cargo nextest run -p panproto-inst -- test_name
The -- separator passes the filter to the test binary. Partial matches work: -- wtype runs all tests with “wtype” in their name.


3.5.6 TypeScript tests
cd sdk/typescript
pnpm test
The TypeScript test suite covers the SDK’s public API, WASM integration, and MessagePack serialization.

title: 3  Building and Testing
text:
3.7 Lint checks
CI enforces strict linting. Run these locally before pushing:
# Rust lints
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings

# Rust formatting
cargo fmt --check

# TypeScript formatting
cd sdk/typescript && npx prettier --check 'src/**/*.ts' 'tests/**/*.ts'
The workspace configures clippy at the pedantic and nursery levels (see [workspace.lints.clippy] in Cargo.toml). If a pedantic lint is a false positive for your code, use a targeted #[allow()] attribute with a comment explaining why.






Noteunsafe_code = "deny"



The workspace denies all unsafe code. panproto achieves its performance through arena allocation (bumpalo), small-vector optimization (smallvec), and hash map selection (rustc-hash) — all within safe Rust.

title: 3  Building and Testing
text:
3.8 CI parity
To replicate exactly what CI runs:
# Step 1: Lint
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check

# Step 2: Test
cargo nextest run --workspace

# Step 3: WASM
wasm-pack build crates/panproto-wasm --target web
wasm-pack test --headless --chrome crates/panproto-wasm

# Step 4: TypeScript
cd sdk/typescript
pnpm install
pnpm build
pnpm test
npx prettier --check 'src/**/*.ts' 'tests/**/*.ts'
If all four steps pass locally, CI will pass.

title: 4  Debugging and Profiling
text:
4.1 Tracing with RUST_LOG
This chapter covers the tools and techniques for diagnosing issues in panproto — from tracing log output through stepping into the migration pipeline to benchmarking with divan.
panproto uses the tracing crate for structured logging. Control verbosity through the RUST_LOG environment variable:

title: 4  Debugging and Profiling
text:
# Debug output for a single crate
RUST_LOG=panproto_inst=debug cargo nextest run -p panproto-inst

# Trace-level output (very verbose) for the migration engine
RUST_LOG=panproto_mig=trace cargo nextest run -p panproto-mig

# Multiple crates at different levels
RUST_LOG=panproto_gat=info,panproto_mig=debug cargo nextest run --workspace

# Everything at debug
RUST_LOG=debug cargo nextest run --workspace






TipFiltering a single test with tracing



Combine RUST_LOG with a test filter to get focused output:
RUST_LOG=panproto_mig=debug cargo nextest run -p panproto-mig -- test_lift_roundtrip

title: 4  Debugging and Profiling
section: 4.2 Stepping through the W-type restrict pipeline
text:
4.2 Stepping through the W-type restrict pipeline
The restrict pipeline in panproto-inst transforms a W-type instance when a migration removes or restructures schema elements. The pipeline has five key functions that serve as natural debugging waypoints:

compute_surviving_set() in crates/panproto-inst/src/wtype.rs — Determines which nodes survive the migration. If your output is missing data, check whether the surviving set is too small.
anchor_nodes() in crates/panproto-inst/src/wtype.rs — Assigns surviving nodes to their new positions in the target schema. If nodes appear at wrong vertices, the anchor computation is the place to look.
contract_ancestors() in crates/panproto-inst/src/wtype.rs — Merges ancestor chains when intermediate vertices are removed. Panics here usually mean the surviving set is inconsistent with the vertex remap.
resolve_edges() in crates/panproto-inst/src/wtype.rs — Rewires edges according to the compiled migration’s edge remap. If edges point to wrong targets, inspect the resolver.
reconstruct_fans() in crates/panproto-inst/src/fan.rs — Rebuilds fan structures (ordered child collections) after restrict. Missing or reordered children point to fan reconstruction issues.

Set RUST_LOG=panproto_inst=debug to see entry/exit traces for each of these functions, including the sizes of intermediate data structures.

title: 4  Debugging and Profiling
text:
4.3 Inspecting WASM boundary data
The WASM boundary (panproto-wasm) serializes all data as MessagePack via rmp-serde. When debugging WASM integration issues, you may need to inspect the raw bytes.

4.3.1 Hex-dump a MessagePack payload
In the TypeScript SDK, add a temporary log before deserialization:
// In sdk/typescript/src/msgpack.ts
console.log('msgpack hex:', Buffer.from(bytes).toString('hex'));
Then decode the hex with a MessagePack inspector (e.g., msgpack.org or the msgpack-tools CLI).


4.3.2 Common deserialization errors








Error
Cause
Fix




InvalidMarkerRead
Empty or truncated payload
Check that the Rust side returned data (not an empty Vec<u8>)


TypeMismatch
Schema version skew between Rust and TS
Rebuild WASM and TS SDK from the same commit


LengthMismatch on arrays
Struct field added/removed on one side
Ensure #[serde(rename)] and TS types match


UnknownVariant
Enum variant added in Rust but not in TS
Add the variant to the TS types.ts discriminated union





4.3.3 Handle-related debugging
The WASM slab allocator (crates/panproto-wasm/src/slab.rs) uses opaque integer handles to reference Rust objects from JavaScript. Common handle issues:

Invalid handle panic: The JS side passed a handle that was never allocated. Check for off-by-one errors or stale handles after a free() call.
Double-free panic: The JS side called free() twice on the same handle. Add a guard in the TS wrapper to nullify handles after freeing.
Memory leak: Handles allocated but never freed. Use the slab_stats() debug API to check the count of live handles.

title: 4  Debugging and Profiling
text:
4.4 Benchmarking with divan
Three crates have benchmarks using the divan framework:
# GAT engine benchmarks (theory construction, morphism checking, colimit)
cargo bench -p panproto-gat

# Instance benchmarks (W-type construction, restrict, parse)
cargo bench -p panproto-inst

# Migration benchmarks (compile, lift, compose)
cargo bench -p panproto-mig

4.4.1 Interpreting output
divan produces output like:
panproto_gat         fastest       │ slowest       │ median        │ mean
├─ colimit_small     142.3 ns      │ 289.1 ns      │ 148.7 ns      │ 152.4 ns
├─ colimit_large     12.41 µs      │ 18.92 µs      │ 13.05 µs      │ 13.28 µs
╰─ morphism_check    84.21 ns      │ 127.6 ns      │ 88.43 ns      │ 90.12 ns
Focus on the median column. Differences under 5% are noise; investigate anything over 10%.


4.4.2 CI regression checks
CI runs benchmarks on every PR and compares against the main branch baseline. If a regression exceeds the threshold (currently 15%), CI reports it as a warning (not a failure). The benchmark data is attached as a PR comment.
To reproduce a CI benchmark comparison locally:
# Baseline on main
git checkout main
cargo bench -p panproto-gat -- --save-baseline main

# Your branch
git checkout your-branch
cargo bench -p panproto-gat -- --baseline main

title: 4  Debugging and Profiling
text:
4.5 Common debugging scenarios

4.5.1 Migration compiles but lift returns wrong data

Check the surviving set. Run with RUST_LOG=panproto_inst=debug and look at the compute_surviving_set output. Are all expected nodes present?
Inspect the vertex remap. Print the compiled migration’s vertex_remap field. Does every source vertex map to the correct target vertex?
Trace the lift. The lift reconstructs source-schema data from a target instance. If it produces wrong values, the resolver functions in the compiled migration may be incorrect.



4.5.2 Existence check passes but restrict panics
The existence check (panproto-mig/src/existence.rs) verifies that a migration can be applied, but it does not run the full restrict pipeline. A panic during restrict usually means:

Resolver coverage gap. The compiled migration has a resolver for every edge in the target schema, but one of them returns an invalid handle. Add a test that calls restrict() directly on a minimal instance.
Fan arity mismatch. A hyper-edge changed its arity (number of targets), but the fan reconstruction did not account for it. Check reconstruct_fans() with tracing enabled.



4.5.3 Property test found a failure but cannot reproduce it

Look for the seed in the failure output: proptest: seed = 0x1234abcd....
Re-run with that seed:
PROPTEST_SEED=0x1234abcd cargo nextest run -p panproto-gat -- test_name
If the test is non-deterministic (it should not be), check for HashMap iteration order sensitivity. panproto uses rustc-hash (FxHashMap) which has a fixed hash seed, so iteration order should be deterministic.



4.5.4 WASM tests pass locally but fail in CI

CI runs WASM tests in headless Chrome. If you are testing locally with Node.js, there may be differences in TextEncoder/TextDecoder behavior.
Reproduce CI conditions: wasm-pack test --headless --chrome crates/panproto-wasm.
If headless Chrome is not installed, use --headless --firefox or install Chrome via your system package manager.



4.5.5 Colimit produces unexpected merged theory
When two theories are merged via colimit() and the result has unexpected sorts or operations:

Verify the span. The colimit is computed over a span: two morphisms from a shared sub-theory. Print both morphisms and confirm that the shared sorts map correctly in each direction.
Check equation preservation. If the merged theory has conflicting equations, colimit() will return an error. But if equations are merely missing, the merged theory may be valid but semantically wrong. Inspect the equations in the output theory.
Trace with debug logging:
RUST_LOG=panproto_gat=debug cargo nextest run -p panproto-gat -- test_colimit
The debug output shows each sort and operation merge step.



4.5.6 Schema validation passes but normalize changes structure
The normalize() function in panproto-schema collapses reference chains and canonicalizes vertex ordering. If your schema looks correct before normalization but wrong after:

Check for reference cycles. normalize() follows reference edges to collapse chains. A cycle causes infinite recursion (which Rust will catch as a stack overflow).
Inspect edge targets. Print the schema’s edges before and after normalization. Look for edges whose targets changed unexpectedly — this usually means a reference chain was collapsed through an intermediate vertex you did not expect.
Run validate before and after: If validate() passes before normalization but fails after, the normalization may have introduced an edge-rule violation. File a bug.

title: 5  Architecture Overview
text:
5.1 The Crate Dependency Hierarchy
This chapter presents the 30,000-foot view of panproto: its crate hierarchy, the key design decisions that shaped the system, and the two-parameter architecture that ties everything together. By the end, you should be able to trace any piece of functionality back to the crate that owns it and understand why the system is structured the way it is.
panproto is organized into eleven crates arranged in six dependency levels. Each level depends only on the levels below it, producing a strict DAG that keeps compile times manageable and enforces separation of concerns.
Level 0 is the mathematical foundation. It knows nothing about schemas, instances, or protocols – only about the algebra of generalized algebraic theories.
Level 1 introduces the data model. panproto-schema defines the hypergraph structure that represents a data format’s type system; panproto-inst defines instances (actual data) conforming to those schemas.
Level 2 is where the interesting operations live: compiling migration specifications into executable form (panproto-mig), constructing bidirectional lenses (panproto-lens), and classifying schema changes as breaking or non-breaking (panproto-check).
Level 3 bundles concrete protocol definitions. Each protocol (AT Protocol, SQL, Protobuf, etc.) is a pair of GATs plus well-formedness rules, all built on the primitives from Levels 0–2. It also provides schema-level presentation functors: the parse/emit operations that convert native format specifications (.proto files, SQL DDL, GraphQL SDL) into abstract Schema models.
Level 3.5 provides the instance-level presentation functors via panproto-io. This crate connects raw data bytes (HTML, CoNLL-U, Protobuf wire messages, JSON documents, CSV rows) to abstract instance models (WInstance/FInstance). Together with panproto-protocols, it completes both levels of the functorial data migration pipeline: schema presentations map format specs to schema models, and instance presentations map format data to instance models. The commutativity guarantee from Spivak 2012 ensures that parse → restrict → emit composes correctly.
Level 4 provides the consumer-facing surfaces: panproto-core re-exports everything through a single dependency, panproto-wasm exposes a handle-based WebAssembly API, and panproto-cli wraps it all in a command-line tool.

5.1.1 The Re-export Facade
panproto-core is the entry point most consumers import. It re-exports all sub-crate APIs under short module names:

/// Re-export of `panproto-gat` for GAT (generalized algebraic theory) types.

/// Re-export of `panproto-inst` for instance representations.

/// Re-export of `panproto-io` for instance-level parse/emit across all protocols.

/// Re-export of `panproto-lens` for bidirectional lens combinators.
pub use panproto_lens as lens;
/// Re-export of `panproto-mig` for migration and lifting operations.

/// Re-export of `panproto-protocols` for built-in protocol definitions.

/// Re-export of `panproto-schema` for schema types and builders.

/// Re-export of `panproto-vcs` for schematic version control.
pub use panproto_vcs as vcs;
This means downstream code writes panproto::gat::Theory rather than importing panproto-gat directly. The facade adds no logic of its own.

title: 5  Architecture Overview
text:
5.2 Key Design Decisions

5.2.1 Why GATs
panproto formalizes data format specifications as generalized algebraic theories (GATs), following the framework introduced by Cartmell (1986). A GAT is a multi-sorted equational theory whose sorts may depend on terms of other sorts. This is precisely the expressive power needed to capture dependent typing relationships that appear in real-world data formats (e.g., “the type of a field depends on a discriminator tag”).
Ordinary algebraic theories in the sense of Lawvere (1963) cannot express sort dependencies, so they are insufficient for modeling protocols like AT Protocol or Protobuf where type structure is genuinely dependent. GATs give us:

Dependent sorts: Hom(a: Ob, b: Ob) in a category theory, or Constraint(v: Vertex) in a schema theory.
Structure-preserving morphisms: maps between theories that respect arities and equations, enabling principled schema migration.
Colimits: pushouts that compose theories modularly, so protocols can be assembled from reusable fragments.







Note



For the user-facing introduction to theories and protocols, see the tutorial chapters 3–4.




5.2.2 Why Handle-Based WASM API
The WASM boundary (panproto-wasm) uses a handle/slab architecture rather than serde-wasm-bindgen serialization. Each Rust object (theory, schema, instance, migration) is stored in a typed slab on the Rust side, and JavaScript receives an opaque u32 handle.
The reason is performance. Serializing a large schema through serde-wasm-bindgen on every API call would dominate the cost of actual computation. With handles, the only data that crosses the WASM boundary is numeric IDs and small MessagePack payloads for results. The TypeScript SDK wraps these handles in ergonomic classes with Symbol.dispose support for deterministic cleanup.


5.2.3 Why MessagePack
panproto uses MessagePack (via rmp-serde) as its primary serialization format for schema and instance data at rest. The choice over JSON, CBOR, or Protobuf-the-format comes down to:

Deterministic serialization: MessagePack’s canonical form guarantees that structurally identical objects produce byte-identical output, which is essential for content-addressed storage and migration hash verification.
Compact binary representation: schemas with hundreds of vertices serialize to kilobytes rather than tens of kilobytes.
Wide ecosystem support: MessagePack libraries exist for every language panproto targets.

JSON is still used as the human-facing format for CLI output and the parse_json/to_json round-trip in panproto-inst.


5.2.4 Why Immutable Fluent Builders
SchemaBuilder and the migration DSL use an immutable fluent pattern: each method consumes self and returns Result<Self, Error>. This prevents partial construction – you cannot hold a reference to a half-built schema and accidentally use it. The pattern also makes validation composable: each step validates its own invariant, and build() only needs to check global properties (like non-emptiness) and compute indices.

title: 5  Architecture Overview
text:
5.3 The Two-Parameter Architecture
Every protocol in panproto is defined by a pair of GATs:

Schema theory – describes the shape of a data format. Its models are schemas (hypergraphs of vertex kinds and edge rules).
Instance theory – describes the content that inhabits a schema. Its models are instances (trees or tables of data).

This factorization is the key architectural insight. A single schema theory can have multiple instance theories (e.g., W-type trees for document data, set-valued functors for relational data), and a single instance theory can serve multiple schema theories. The Protocol struct in panproto-schema captures this pairing:
Protocol = (schema_theory: GAT, instance_theory: GAT, edge_rules, ...)
Migration between protocol versions is then a morphism at the schema theory level, compiled into executable operations at the instance theory level. The mathematical guarantee is that restriction along a theory morphism is functorial – it composes correctly and preserves the structural axioms of the instance theory (Spivak 2012).

title: 5  Architecture Overview
text:
5.4 Migration Lifecycle
The following sequence diagram traces the full lifecycle from defining a protocol through migrating data:






sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant IO as panproto-io
    participant Protocols as panproto-protocols
    participant Schema as panproto-schema
    participant Check as panproto-check
    participant Mig as panproto-mig
    participant Inst as panproto-inst

    User->>Protocols: Define protocol (schema theory + instance theory)
    User->>Schema: Build schema v1 via SchemaBuilder
    User->>Schema: Build schema v2 via SchemaBuilder
    User->>Check: check_existence(v1, v2)
    Check-->>User: ExistenceReport (added/removed/modified)
    User->>Mig: compile(migration_spec)
    Mig-->>User: CompiledMigration
    User->>IO: parse(protocol, schema_v1, raw_bytes)
    IO-->>User: WInstance / FInstance (source)
    User->>Inst: wtype_restrict(instance, v1, v2, compiled)
    Inst-->>User: Migrated WInstance
    User->>IO: emit(protocol, schema_v2, migrated_instance)
    IO-->>User: Raw bytes (target format)




Figure 5.2: Full migration lifecycle, from raw data through migration to target format.





The pipeline is:

Define protocols – select or compose GATs from the panproto-protocols registry.
Build schemas – construct v1 and v2 using SchemaBuilder, which validates each element against the protocol’s edge rules.
Check existence – use panproto-check to classify the differences between v1 and v2 (what was added, removed, modified).
Compile migration – panproto-mig compiles a migration specification into a CompiledMigration containing vertex remaps, edge remaps, and contraction resolvers.
Lift data – panproto-inst executes the 5-step wtype_restrict pipeline to project instance data from v1 to v2.

title: 6  panproto-gat – The GAT Engine
text:
6.1 Sorts and Sort Parameters
panproto-gat is Level 0 of the panproto crate hierarchy: the mathematical foundation on which everything else is built. This crate implements generalized algebraic theories (GATs) as defined by Cartmell (1986), providing the data structures and algorithms for sorts, operations, equations, theory morphisms, and colimits.
This chapter walks through every major type and function in the crate, explaining both the Rust implementation and the mathematical concepts it encodes.
A sort is a type in a GAT. Sorts may be simple (zero parameters) or dependent (parameterized by terms of other sorts). The distinction between simple and dependent sorts is what separates GATs from ordinary algebraic theories in the sense of Lawvere (1963): in an ordinary algebraic theory, all sorts are simple.

title: 6  panproto-gat – The GAT Engine
6  panproto-gat – The GAT Engine
6  panproto-gat – The GAT Engine
text:
6.3 Equations
Equations (axioms) constrain the behavior of operations. An equation consists of a name and two Term trees that must be considered equal. panproto uses a simple term language:

Term::Var(name) – a variable reference.
Term::App(op_name, args) – application of an operation to arguments.

Equations are not computationally evaluated; they serve as proof obligations that theory morphisms must preserve. When panproto-gat checks a morphism, it verifies that for each equation lhs = rhs in the domain theory, the mapped equation F(lhs) = F(rhs) exists in the codomain theory.

The GATlab system (Lynch et al. 2024) takes a similar approach to implementing GATs in Julia, focusing on computational category theory. panproto’s panproto-gat is more narrowly scoped – it provides just enough GAT machinery to support schema migration and protocol composition, without attempting to be a general-purpose categorical algebra system.






Important



panproto-gat is the only crate in the system that has zero knowledge of schemas, instances, or protocols. If you find yourself importing panproto-schema types in panproto-gat, something has gone wrong architecturally. The abstraction boundary here is strict and intentional.

title: 7  panproto-schema – Schemas and Protocols
text:
7.1 Schema Elements
panproto-schema is a Level 1 crate that defines the data structures for schema graphs and the protocols that govern their construction. A schema is a model of a protocol’s schema theory GAT: it instantiates the abstract sorts (Vertex, Edge, Constraint, …) with concrete elements that describe a particular data format version. A protocol is the configuration object that says which GATs to use and what well-formedness rules to enforce.
This chapter covers every major type in the crate and the validation pipeline that ensures schemas are well-formed at construction time.
A schema is a labeled, directed hypergraph. The four element types – Vertex, Edge, HyperEdge, and Constraint – correspond to sorts in the schema theory GAT. Each element carries enough metadata for the builder to validate it against the protocol’s rules.

title: 7  panproto-schema – Schemas and Protocols
7  panproto-schema – Schemas and Protocols
7  panproto-schema – Schemas and Protocols
7  panproto-schema – Schemas and Protocols
7  panproto-schema – Schemas and Protocols
text:
7.5 Open vs. Closed Protocols
The builder supports both open and closed protocols:

Closed protocol: defines obj_kinds and/or edge_rules. Only vertex kinds and edge kinds explicitly mentioned are accepted. This is the default for production protocols like AT Protocol and SQL.
Open protocol: has empty obj_kinds and edge_rules. Any vertex kind and any edge kind are accepted without validation. This is useful for testing and for protocols where the set of kinds is not known ahead of time.

The distinction is handled by a guard clause in the builder: validation is skipped when the protocol has no declarations to validate against.

title: 7  panproto-schema – Schemas and Protocols
text:
7.6 Schema Normalization
The normalize module (in schema/src/normalize.rs) provides canonical ordering of schema elements for deterministic serialization. Normalization sorts vertices by ID, edges by (src, tgt, kind, name), and constraints by (sort, value). This ensures that structurally identical schemas produce byte-identical MessagePack output, which is critical for content-addressed storage and migration hash verification.

title: 7  panproto-schema – Schemas and Protocols
text:
7.7 Schema Validation
Beyond the per-element checks in the builder, the validate module provides post-build validation:

Constraint sort validation: checks that every constraint’s sort field appears in the protocol’s constraint_sorts list.
Connectivity checks: verifies that the schema graph is connected (every vertex is reachable from some root vertex via edges).
Required-edge checks: verifies that vertices which declare required edges actually have those edges in the schema.

These checks are separated from the builder because they require the complete schema to evaluate. The builder validates local invariants (per-vertex, per-edge); the validator checks global invariants (connectivity, completeness).

title: 7  panproto-schema – Schemas and Protocols
text:
7.8 Design Rationale

7.8.1 Why Adjacency Indices at Build Time
Computing adjacency indices eagerly at build time (rather than lazily on first access) is a deliberate trade-off. Schema construction happens once; schema traversal happens many times during migration compilation, instance parsing, and validation. Paying the O(E) index-building cost once eliminates repeated O(E) scans during traversal.


7.8.2 Why String-Keyed Maps
Vertices, edges, and constraints are identified by String keys rather than integer IDs. This is intentional: string IDs carry semantic meaning (e.g., "post:body.text") that makes schemas human-readable and debuggable. The performance cost is acceptable because schema sizes are modest (hundreds to low thousands of elements) and all hot-path lookups go through the precomputed HashMap indices.


7.8.3 Why Edge in Adjacency Values
The adjacency indices store full Edge clones rather than references or indices into a flat edge array. This avoids lifetime complications and makes the adjacency API simple (outgoing_edges returns &[Edge]). The memory cost of edge duplication is offset by the SmallVec inline storage optimization.

title: 8  panproto-inst – Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline
text:
8.1 W-types: The Mathematical Background
panproto-inst is a Level 1 crate that defines how actual data is represented and how it is migrated between schema versions. The crate provides two instance representations – W-type trees for document/hierarchical data and set-valued functors for relational data – along with the algorithms that project instances along compiled migrations.
This chapter focuses on the W-type representation and its 5-step restrict pipeline, which is the primary data migration algorithm in panproto.
The name “W-type” comes from the theory of wellfounded trees in dependent type theory (Gambino and Hyland 2004). A W-type is an inductive type defined by a signature: a type of positions (nodes) and, for each position, a type of arities (branching structure). In panproto’s encoding:
This is a faithful computational implementation of the containers of Abbott et al. (2005): a W-type instance is a container whose shape is prescribed by a schema. The restrict pipeline is then a morphism of containers induced by the schema migration.
The functorial data migration framework of Spivak (2012) provides the categorical semantics: restriction along a theory morphism is a functor from instances of the target schema to instances of the source schema. panproto’s wtype_restrict is the concrete implementation of this functor for the W-type instance theory.
crumbs: 8  panproto-inst -- Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline

title: 8  panproto-inst – Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline
text:
Positions are schema vertices – each node in the instance tree is anchored to a vertex in the schema.
Arities are determined by the outgoing edges of the anchor vertex – the children of a node correspond to the schema edges leaving its anchor.

title: 8  panproto-inst – Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline
text:
8.5 Functor Instances (functor_restrict)
panproto-inst also provides an alternative instance representation for relational (tabular) data: FInstance, a set-valued functor. Each schema vertex maps to a table (a Vec of row maps), and each edge maps to foreign-key relationships.
The restrict operation for functor instances is much simpler than the W-type pipeline. It is precomposition (denoted Delta_F in the functorial data migration literature): for each vertex in the target schema, pull the corresponding table from the source instance via the migration’s vertex map.
This operation is the computational realization of the pullback functor described by Spivak (2012). It composes correctly by construction: restricting along a composed migration is the same as restricting twice.






Tip



Use FInstance and functor_restrict for relational protocols (SQL, MongoDB). Use WInstance and wtype_restrict for document protocols (AT Protocol, JSON Schema, OpenAPI). The choice is determined by the protocol’s instance_theory field.
crumbs: 8  panproto-inst -- Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline

title: 8  panproto-inst – Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline
text:
8.6 JSON Round-Trip (parse_json / to_json)
The parse module provides schema-guided JSON parsing and serialization:

parse_json(schema, root_vertex, json_value) – recursively walks a JSON value, creating a WInstance tree. At each level, it consults the schema’s outgoing edges to determine which JSON fields correspond to child nodes and which are leaf values.
to_json(schema, instance) – reconstructs JSON from a WInstance by walking the tree from the root, using edge names as property keys.

The round-trip preserves:

Structural fidelity: every schema-recognized field is correctly nested.
Extra fields: JSON properties not recognized by the schema are stored in Node::extra_fields and re-emitted on serialization.
Discriminators: $type tags on union-typed objects are preserved via Node::discriminator.

This round-trip is essential for migration scenarios: parse JSON with the source schema, restrict, then serialize with the target schema.
crumbs: 8  panproto-inst -- Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline

title: 8  panproto-inst – Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline
text:
8.7 Validation Axioms (I1–I7)
The validate module checks that a WInstance satisfies seven structural axioms. These axioms formalize the well-formedness conditions for W-type instances:



Table 8.1: The seven W-type validation axioms.










Axiom
Check
Description




I1
check_anchors
All node anchors exist in the schema


I2
check_edges
All arc edges exist in the schema


I3
check_root
Root node exists in the node set


I4
check_reachability
All nodes reachable from root via arcs


I5
check_required_edges
Required edges present for each node


I6
check_parent_map
Parent map consistent with arc list


I7
check_fans
Fan parent and children exist; hyper-edge in schema






validate_wtype(schema, instance) returns a Vec<ValidationError> – an empty vector means the instance is valid. Collecting all violations (rather than failing on the first) supports diagnostic tooling: the CLI can report all problems at once.






Important



The restrict pipeline is designed to preserve axioms I1–I7. If the source instance is valid and the compiled migration is well-formed, the restricted instance is guaranteed to be valid with respect to the target schema. This is the correctness theorem that the functorial framework provides.



8.7.1 Axiom Preservation Through the Pipeline
Each step of the restrict pipeline maintains or establishes specific axioms:

Step 1 (anchor_surviving) ensures that all surviving nodes have valid anchors in the target schema (establishing I1 for the restricted set).
Step 2 (reachable_from_root) establishes I4: only nodes reachable from the root survive.
Step 3 (ancestor_contraction) re-establishes the tree structure after intermediate nodes are pruned.
Step 4 (resolve_edge) establishes I2: all new arcs correspond to edges in the target schema.
Step 5 (reconstruct_fans) establishes I7: all fans reference valid hyper-edges and nodes.

I3 (root exists) is checked explicitly – if the root is pruned, the pipeline returns an error rather than producing an invalid instance.

title: 8  panproto-inst – Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline
text:
8.8 Instance Lifecycle
Putting it all together, the typical lifecycle of a W-type instance is:

Parse: Create a WInstance from raw data via one of two pathways:

Format-specific (recommended): panproto_io::parse_wtype("html", schema_v1, &raw_bytes) — uses SIMD-accelerated format-specific parsers for maximum throughput.
Generic JSON: parse_json(schema_v1, root, &json_value) — for data already in JSON form.

Validate: validate_wtype(schema_v1, instance) confirms well-formedness.
Restrict: wtype_restrict(instance, schema_v1, schema_v2, migration) projects to the new schema.
Validate (optional): validate_wtype(schema_v2, restricted) confirms the result.
Serialize: Emit the migrated instance via:

Format-specific: panproto_io::emit_wtype("html", schema_v2, &restricted) — native format output.
Generic JSON: to_json(schema_v2, restricted) — produces JSON.


Steps 2 and 4 are optional in production (the pipeline is designed to preserve validity), but recommended during development and testing.






Note



The panproto-io crate provides format-specific parsers for all 77 supported protocols. The generic parse_json/to_json functions in this crate remain useful as the foundation for panproto-io’s JSON pathway and for direct JSON manipulation in tests and tooling.
crumbs: 8  panproto-inst -- Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline

title: 8  panproto-inst – Instances and the W-type Restrict Pipeline
text:
8.9 Design Rationale

8.9.1 Why Separate Node IDs from Anchors
Node IDs are u32 values, while anchors are String schema vertex IDs. Multiple nodes can share the same anchor (e.g., an array of strings all anchored to the same "items" vertex). Keeping IDs numeric and anchors semantic gives O(1) node lookup by ID while preserving the schema-level meaning through the anchor.


8.9.2 Why Precomputed Parent and Children Maps
The parent_map and children_map are computed once in WInstance::new() and never updated. This is safe because WInstance is immutable after construction. The restrict pipeline creates a new WInstance rather than mutating the source.


8.9.3 Why Five Steps Instead of One
Decomposing the restrict algorithm into five independent functions serves two purposes:

Testability: each step can be unit-tested in isolation with small, purpose-built instances. The test suite in wtype.rs tests each step individually before testing the composed pipeline.
Debuggability: when a migration produces unexpected results, you can inspect the output of each step to locate the problem. The CLI’s --verbose mode logs intermediate results at each step.

The five-step decomposition also mirrors the mathematical structure: signature restriction (Step 1) is the pullback on objects, reachability (Step 2) enforces the wellfoundedness condition, contraction (Step 3) computes the pushforward on the tree, edge resolution (Step 4) lifts the tree morphism to the arc level, and fan reconstruction (Step 5) handles the higher-dimensional structure.






Abbott, Michael, Thorsten Altenkirch, and Neil Ghani. 2005. “Containers: Constructing Strictly Positive Types.” Theoretical Computer Science 342 (1): 3–27. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tcs.2005.06.002.


Gambino, Nicola, and J. Martin E. Hyland. 2004. “Wellfounded Trees and Dependent Polynomial Functors.” Types for Proofs and Programs (TYPES 2003), Lecture notes in computer science, vol. 3085: 210–25. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-540-24849-1_14.


Spivak, David I. 2012. “Functorial Data Migration.” Information and Computation 217: 31–51. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ic.2012.05.001.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.1 The Migration Struct
The panproto-mig crate is the core migration engine for panproto. It computes, validates, and applies schema migrations—transforming instances from one schema version to another while preserving data integrity through theory morphisms. This chapter walks through the full migration pipeline, from specification to execution.
If you have worked with database migrations or API versioning tools before, panproto’s migration engine will feel conceptually familiar. The key difference is that panproto derives its validation rules from the protocol’s algebraic theory rather than hardcoding them, making the same engine work across all 60+ supported protocols.
The crate lives at crates/panproto-mig and is published as panproto-mig on crates.io. It depends on panproto-schema for schema types, panproto-gat for theory types, and panproto-inst for compiled migration and instance types.
A Migration describes a mapping between two schemas. It encodes the graph morphism (vertex and edge maps), hyper-edge maps for protocols with multi-arity relations, and resolvers for disambiguation when intermediate vertices are contracted.
The six fields break down into three logical groups:
Core morphism. The vertex_map and edge_map fields define the fundamental graph morphism from source schema to target schema. Every source vertex that should survive in the target gets an entry in vertex_map; vertices absent from the map are dropped. Similarly, edge_map records how source edges correspond to target edges.
Hyper-edge morphism. The hyper_edge_map and label_map fields handle protocols whose schema theories include the HyperEdge sort (Group B protocols such as SQL, Parquet, and Cassandra). A hyper-edge connects multiple vertices via labeled positions, so the mapping must track both the hyper-edge identity and the label assignments.
Contraction resolvers. The resolver and hyper_resolver fields handle the case where a migration contracts intermediate vertices. For example, if a schema has the path A -> B -> C and the migration drops B, the resulting schema connects A directly to C. But there may be multiple edges between A and C in the target—the resolver records which one to use. The hyper_resolver does the same for hyper-edge contraction, additionally providing label remaps.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.2 The Migration Pipeline
The full pipeline for applying a migration follows five stages:





sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Mig as Migration
    participant Exist as check_existence
    participant Comp as compile()
    participant CM as CompiledMigration
    participant Lift as lift_wtype / lift_functor

    User->>Mig: define vertex_map, edge_map, ...
    Mig->>Exist: check_existence(protocol, src, tgt, mig, registry)
    Exist-->>Mig: ExistenceReport { valid, errors }
    Mig->>Comp: compile(src, tgt, migration)
    Comp-->>CM: CompiledMigration
    CM->>Lift: lift_wtype(instance, compiled) or lift_functor(...)
    Lift-->>User: transformed instance







Existence checking (check_existence): validates that the migration is well-formed with respect to the protocol’s theories. This is the “can we even do this?” step.
Compilation (compile): pre-computes surviving sets, remapping tables, and resolvers for fast per-record application. This is the “prepare the execution plan” step.
Lifting (lift_wtype, lift_functor): applies the compiled migration to concrete W-type or functor instances. This is the “execute” step.
Composition (compose): chains two sequential migrations into a single equivalent migration. This is the “optimize multi-step evolution” step.
Inversion (invert): constructs the inverse migration when the original is bijective. This is the “undo” step.

Stages 1–3 form the critical path for applying a single migration. Stages 4 and 5 are used when managing sequences of migrations or implementing rollback capabilities.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.4 Compilation
The compile function translates a Migration specification into a CompiledMigration—a form optimized for per-record application. Compilation pre-computes:

Surviving vertex set: the image of vertex_map in the target schema. Only nodes anchored at these vertices will be kept during lifting.
Surviving edge set: the image of edge_map. Only arcs corresponding to these edges will be preserved.
Vertex and edge remap tables: maps from source IDs to target IDs. These allow the lifter to update anchors and edge labels in O(1) per element.
Resolver copies: the contraction resolvers carried forward for use during lifting. When the lifter encounters two surviving nodes that were not directly connected in the source, it consults the resolver to determine which target edge to use.

The compiled form enables lift_wtype and lift_functor to process each record in O(nodes) time without re-deriving the migration structure on every invocation. For high-throughput scenarios (e.g., migrating millions of ATProto records), this separation between compilation and execution is critical for performance.






Tip



If your migration has already passed existence checking, compilation will not fail unless the migration references vertices or edges absent from the target schema (which existence checking should have caught). In practice, always run existence checking first—it produces much more informative error messages than compilation failures.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.5 Lifting
Lifting applies a compiled migration to a concrete instance. panproto supports two lifting modes corresponding to the two families of instance theories:

9.5.1 W-Type Lifting
lift_wtype handles tree-shaped instances used by JSON-like, XML-like, and document protocols. The algorithm:

Walks the source instance’s node tree from the root.
For each node, checks whether its anchor vertex is in the surviving set.
If surviving: remaps the anchor to its target vertex ID and copies the node to the result.
If dropped: omits the node. Any children anchored at surviving vertices are re-parented using the resolver.
Rebuilds the arc list and fan list based on surviving nodes and the edge remap table.

The resolver is critical during step 4. When a dropped intermediate vertex B sat between A and C, the lifter needs to create a direct arc from A to C. If multiple edges exist between A and C in the target schema, the resolver disambiguates.


9.5.2 Functor Lifting
lift_functor handles tabular instances used by relational and columnar protocols. The algorithm:

Iterates over tables in the source instance.
For each table, checks whether its anchor vertex is in the surviving set.
If surviving: copies all rows, remapping foreign keys according to the vertex remap table and dropping columns for non-surviving edges.
If dropped: omits the entire table.

Both lifting functions delegate to the corresponding restrict operations in panproto-inst, which provide the low-level tree/table manipulation.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.6 Composition and Inversion

9.6.1 Composition
Composition chains two migrations \(m_1: S_1 \to S_2\) and \(m_2: S_2 \to S_3\) into a single migration \(m_2 \circ m_1: S_1 \to S_3\). The composed vertex map is the functional composition of the two vertex maps: for each source vertex \(v\) in \(S_1\), the composed map sends \(v\) to \(m_2(m_1(v))\). The edge and hyper-edge maps compose similarly.
Resolvers from both migrations are merged, with \(m_2\)’s resolvers taking precedence when keys overlap. This is correct because the second migration’s resolvers encode decisions about the final target schema, which supersede the intermediate schema’s structure.
Composition is associative: \((m_3 \circ m_2) \circ m_1 = m_3 \circ (m_2 \circ m_1)\). Combined with the identity migration as the unit, this makes migrations a category—the migration category over a fixed protocol.


9.6.2 Inversion
Inversion constructs \(m^{-1}: S_2 \to S_1\) from a migration \(m: S_1 \to S_2\), but only when \(m\) is bijective. Specifically, it requires:

Injectivity of vertex_map: no two source vertices map to the same target vertex. If user.name and user.displayName both map to user.label, the inverse cannot know which to restore.
Surjectivity of vertex_map: every target vertex has a preimage. If the target has a vertex that no source vertex maps to, the inverse has no source for it.
The same conditions on edge_map and hyper_edge_map.

If any condition fails, invert returns an InvertError identifying the specific obstruction.






Important



Inversion requires bijectivity. A migration that drops vertices or contracts edges is not invertible. Use the lens system (Chapter 10) when you need round-trip transformations for non-bijective mappings.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.7 Relationship to Category Theory
The migration pipeline implements the categorical notion of a graph morphism between C-sets (copresheaf instances) over a schema theory (Spivak 2012). Existence checking validates the naturality conditions of this morphism—the requirement that the mapping commutes with the schema’s operations. Compilation pre-evaluates the action on objects. Lifting is the functorial action on instances (the pushforward along the schema morphism).
Composition corresponds to composition of natural transformations. The theory-derived existence conditions generalize the gluing and dangling conditions from algebraic graph rewriting (Ehrig et al. 2006; Löwe 1993). Where DPO rewriting checks these conditions for a specific rewriting rule, panproto checks them for an arbitrary migration between two schemas under an arbitrary GAT-based theory.
For a hands-on walkthrough of building and applying migrations, see tutorial chapters 5–6.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.8 Worked Example: Renaming a Field
To make the pipeline concrete, consider renaming the text field to content in a schema with vertices {body, body.text} and an edge body -> body.text with name "text".
Step 1: Define the migration.
The vertex_map maps body to body and body.text to body.content. The edge_map maps the old edge to a new edge with name "content". No resolvers are needed because no vertices are dropped.
Step 2: Check existence.
check_existence looks up the protocol’s schema theory. If the theory has a Constraint sort, it verifies that any constraints on body.text are compatible with the constraints on body.content in the target. Since this is a rename (same kind, same constraints), all checks pass.
Step 3: Compile.
compile produces a CompiledMigration with:

surviving_verts: {body, body.content}
surviving_edges: {body -> body.content (content)}
vertex_remap: {body.text -> body.content}
edge_remap: old edge maps to new edge

Step 4: Lift.
lift_wtype walks the source instance. Nodes anchored at body keep their anchor. Nodes anchored at body.text have their anchor remapped to body.content. Arcs are updated to use the new edge. The result is a valid instance under the target schema.
This is a bijective migration, so invert would also succeed, producing a migration that renames content back to text.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.9 Interaction with Other Crates
panproto-mig sits at the center of panproto’s architecture:

panproto-schema provides the Schema, Edge, and Protocol types that migrations operate on.
panproto-gat provides the Theory type used for existence checking and the colimit function used by panproto-protocols.
panproto-inst provides CompiledMigration, WInstance, and the wtype_restrict/functor_restrict operations that lifting delegates to.
panproto-lens wraps CompiledMigration in a Lens and adds complement tracking for non-bijective transformations.
panproto-check uses diff to detect changes between schemas, which can be combined with migration information.

Understanding these dependencies helps when debugging: if existence checking fails, the issue is in the migration specification or the theory registry. If compilation fails, the issue is in the schema. If lifting fails, the issue is in the instance or the compiled migration.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.10 Performance Considerations
The migration pipeline is designed for throughput:

Existence checking is O(|vertex_map| + |edge_map| + |hyper_edge_map|) for the universal checks, plus O(|constraints|) for the conditional constraint check and O(|hyper_edges| * |labels|) for the conditional signature coherence check. This is fast even for large schemas.
Compilation is O(|migration|) and happens once per schema version pair.
Lifting is O(|instance nodes|) per record, with hash-map lookups for remap and resolver operations. For W-type instances, this is a single tree traversal. For functor instances, this is a single pass over rows.
Composition is O(|vertex_map| + |edge_map|) and happens once when chaining migrations.

For batch migration scenarios (e.g., migrating a million ATProto records from schema v1 to v2), the cost is dominated by lifting, which is linear in the total data size.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.11 Error Handling
The panproto-mig error types are designed to provide actionable diagnostics:

ExistenceError: includes variants for WellFormedness (invalid vertex/edge references), ConstraintTightened, RequiredFieldMissing, KindInconsistency, SignatureCoherence, Simultaneity, ReachabilityRisk, and EdgeMissing. Each variant carries enough context to identify the exact source of the problem.
ComposeError: reports when composition fails due to incompatible intermediate schemas.
InvertError: identifies the specific bijectivity violation (non-injective vertex map, non-surjective edge map, etc.).
LiftError: reports runtime failures during instance transformation.

All error types implement Display for human-readable messages and Serialize/Deserialize for machine consumption.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.12 Summary









Stage
Function
Input
Output




Validate
check_existence
Migration + protocol + schemas + theory registry
ExistenceReport


Compile
compile
Migration + schemas
CompiledMigration


Apply
lift_wtype / lift_functor
CompiledMigration + instance
Transformed instance


Chain
compose
Two Migrations
Single Migration


Reverse
invert
Migration
Migration (if bijective)



The key insight of panproto-mig is that the migration engine does not need to know the details of any specific protocol. The theory registry tells it which structural invariants to check, and the compiled migration tells the lifter which elements to keep, drop, or remap. This separation is what allows panproto to support 60+ protocols with a single migration engine.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.13 Module Map
The crate is organized into the following modules:








Module
Exports
Purpose




migration
Migration
Migration specification type with vertex/edge/hyper-edge maps and resolvers


existence
ExistenceReport, check_existence
Theory-derived validation of migration well-formedness


compile
compile
Pre-computation of surviving sets and remap tables


lift
lift_wtype, lift_functor
Instance transformation for W-type and functor instances


compose
