invert
invert
Construction of inverse migrations for bijective mappings


error
ExistenceError, ComposeError, InvertError, LiftError, MigError
Error types for each pipeline stage



All public types are re-exported from the crate root, so consumers can write use panproto_mig::Migration without navigating the module structure.

title: 9  panproto-mig — The Migration Engine
text:
9.14 Testing Strategy
The crate’s test suite covers each pipeline stage independently:

Existence checking tests verify that specific error variants are produced for known-bad migrations (constraint tightening, kind inconsistency, required field missing, etc.). Each test constructs a minimal schema pair and migration, registers the appropriate theories, and asserts that the expected error variant appears in the report.
Compilation tests verify that surviving sets and remap tables are computed correctly for various migration shapes.
Lifting tests use small hand-crafted instances to verify that nodes are correctly dropped, remapped, and re-parented.
Composition tests verify that composing two migrations produces the same result as applying them sequentially.
Inversion tests verify that invertible migrations round-trip correctly and that non-invertible migrations produce the expected InvertError.

The test suite is intentionally structured so that each test constructs its own minimal schema pair and migration from scratch, avoiding shared test fixtures that could mask bugs through accidental coupling. See Chapter 17 for the project-wide testing conventions.
Property-based tests (using proptest) supplement the hand-crafted cases, generating random schemas and migrations to verify invariants like “composing a migration with its inverse yields an identity migration” and “existence checking accepts the identity migration for any schema.”






Ehrig, Hartmut, Karsten Ehrig, Ulrike Prange, and Gabriele Taentzer. 2006. Fundamentals of Algebraic Graph Transformation. Monographs in Theoretical Computer Science. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/3-540-31188-2.


Löwe, Michael. 1993. “Algebraic Approach to Single-Pushout Graph Transformation.” Theoretical Computer Science 109 (1–2): 181–224. https://doi.org/10.1016/0304-3975(93)90068-5.


Spivak, David I. 2012. “Functorial Data Migration.” Information and Computation 217: 31–51. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ic.2012.05.001.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.1 Why Lenses?
The panproto-lens crate implements bidirectional lenses for panproto, enabling round-trip transformations between schema versions even when the mapping is not bijective. Where panproto-mig requires bijectivity for inversion, lenses track a complement—the data discarded during the forward direction—so that the backward direction can reconstruct the original source from a (possibly modified) view.
This chapter covers the asymmetric lens primitives (get and put), the complement type, Cambria-style combinators for building lenses declaratively, and the lens laws that guarantee correctness.
The crate lives at crates/panproto-lens and is published as panproto-lens on crates.io. It depends on panproto-schema for schema types, panproto-inst for W-type instances and compiled migrations, and panproto-mig for migration composition. It is a Level 2 crate in panproto’s dependency hierarchy.
Migrations (Chapter 9) handle the common case where a schema evolution preserves all information—renames, reorderings, and bijective restructurings. But many real-world evolutions are lossy: removing a deprecated field, collapsing two fields into one, or changing a field’s type. These transformations cannot be inverted by panproto-mig because there is no unique preimage.
Lenses solve this by capturing the “lost” information in a complement. The forward direction (get) produces a view of the data under the new schema plus a complement recording everything that was discarded. The backward direction (put) takes a (possibly modified) view and the stored complement, and reconstructs the original data.
This makes lenses the right tool for:

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
Schema downgrades: projecting data from a newer schema version to an older one while retaining enough information to upgrade back.
Cross-protocol bridges: converting between protocols where the target has fewer fields than the source.
Selective views: exposing a subset of a record to an API consumer while preserving the full record server-side.
Incremental adoption: migrating to a new schema incrementally, with the complement serving as a rollback mechanism during the transition period.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.4 Backward Direction: put
The put function restores a source instance from a (possibly modified) view and the stored complement. The reconstruction proceeds in stages:

Copy view nodes: all nodes from the view are copied, with their anchors un-remapped from target vertex IDs back to source vertex IDs using the inverse of the compiled migration’s vertex remap.
Re-insert dropped nodes: every node recorded in the complement’s dropped_nodes is inserted back into the node set.
Rebuild arcs from original parents: for each surviving node, the original_parent mapping tells us who its parent was in the source. The function finds the appropriate edge (consulting contraction_choices for disambiguation) and creates an arc.
Re-insert dropped arcs: arcs from the complement are added back, provided both endpoints are present in the restored node set.
Reconstruct fans: view fans are un-remapped; dropped fans whose nodes are all present are re-inserted.

The result is a full source instance that incorporates any edits made to the view while preserving the structural elements that were invisible to the view.






Note



If the view was modified between get and put (e.g., a field value was changed by an API consumer), those modifications are preserved in the restored instance. The complement provides the structure that the view cannot represent; the view provides the content that may have been updated.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.5 The get/put Round-Trip
The following diagram illustrates the data flow through a complete round-trip:





flowchart LR
    S[Source Instance] -->|get| V[View]
    S -->|get| C[Complement]
    V -->|put| R[Restored Instance]
    C -->|put| R
    R ---|should equal| S






The complement acts as a side channel, carrying exactly the information that the view cannot represent. Together, the view and complement contain all the information in the source—just partitioned differently.
Consider a concrete example. Suppose a source schema has fields {name, email, age} and the target schema has only {name, email}. The get direction produces a view with {name, email} and a complement containing the age node, its arc from the parent, and any constraints. If an API consumer updates email in the view, calling put with the modified view and the original complement produces a source instance with the updated email, the original name, and the original age—nothing is lost.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.6 Lens Laws
panproto lenses satisfy two laws that jointly guarantee correctness:
GetPut law: applying put to the result of get recovers the original source.
\[\text{put}(\text{get}(s).\text{view},\; \text{get}(s).\text{complement}) = s\]
This law says that get followed by put is a no-op: the round-trip preserves the source exactly. If this law were violated, data would be silently lost or corrupted during the round-trip.
PutGet law: applying get to the result of put recovers the view and complement.
\[\text{get}(\text{put}(v, c)) = (v, c)\]
This law says that put followed by get is also a no-op: restoring a source from a view and complement, then projecting it again, yields the same view and complement. If this law were violated, put would introduce phantom data that get would then discard, or put would fail to fully incorporate the complement.

10.6.1 Property Testing
These laws are property-tested in panproto-lens via the check_laws function, which:

Generates random source schemas using the protocol’s theory.
Generates random source instances conforming to those schemas.
Constructs lenses from random combinator chains.
Verifies both GetPut and PutGet for each generated triple.

The property tests run as part of the standard cargo test suite and also in the CI pipeline with extended iteration counts.






Note



The lens laws follow the formulation of asymmetric lenses from Foster et al. (2007), adapted to the setting of W-type instances over GAT-based schema theories. The complement corresponds to the abstract complement in the lens framework of Johnson and Rosebrugh (2013).

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.8 Design Rationale
The asymmetric lens design follows the theoretical framework of Hofmann et al. (2011), which establishes that complement-based lenses form a category: identity lenses exist, and lenses compose associatively. panproto exploits this by allowing lens composition through Compose combinators and through direct composition of compiled migrations.
The choice of asymmetric (rather than symmetric) lenses is deliberate. In protocol evolution, one direction is typically authoritative—the new schema version defines the “view,” and the old schema’s extra structure is captured as complement. Symmetric lenses (Hofmann et al. 2011) would require both sides to carry complements, adding complexity without clear benefit for the schema migration use case.
The complement-based approach also integrates naturally with panproto’s existing infrastructure. A Lens wraps a CompiledMigration (the same type used by panproto-mig), so the forward direction of a lens is exactly a migration lift. The complement is the additional information needed to make the transformation reversible.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
section: 10.9 Worked Example: Removing a Deprecated Field
text:
10.9 Worked Example: Removing a Deprecated Field
Consider a schema with vertices {post, post.text, post.legacy_id} and edges from post to both children. The legacy_id field is deprecated and should be removed from the API view, but existing data in storage must retain it.
Step 1: Define the lens via combinators.
let combinators = &[Combinator::RemoveField {
    name: "legacy_id".to_string(),
}];
let lens = from_combinators(&src_schema, combinators, &protocol)?;
The from_combinators function derives the target schema (with legacy_id removed) and the compiled migration automatically.
Step 2: Get the view.
Calling get(&lens, &source_instance) produces:

View: an instance with nodes anchored at post and post.text only.
Complement: contains the post.legacy_id node, the arc from post to post.legacy_id, and any associated values.

Step 3: Modify the view (optional).
An API consumer updates post.text to a new value. The view now differs from the original.
Step 4: Put it back.
Calling put(&lens, &modified_view, &complement) produces a restored source instance with all three fields: post, post.text (with the updated value), and post.legacy_id (with the original value from the complement).
The GetPut and PutGet laws guarantee this round-trip is correct regardless of the specific data values involved.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.10 Complement Storage Strategies
In production deployments, the complement must be persisted alongside the view. Common strategies include:

Database column: store the serialized complement as a JSONB or BLOB column alongside the view data. This is the simplest approach and works well when the complement is small relative to the view.
Companion file: for file-based storage (e.g., Parquet datasets), store the complement in a sidecar file with a .complement.json extension.
Metadata header: for protocols with metadata support (e.g., HTTP responses), encode the complement in a custom header or trailer.
Separate table: for relational databases, store complements in a dedicated table keyed by record ID and schema version. This avoids schema modifications to the main table.

The Complement type derives Serialize and Deserialize, so any serde-compatible format works: JSON, MessagePack, CBOR, etc.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.11 Interaction with Breaking Change Detection
Lenses and breaking change detection (Chapter 11) are complementary:

A RemoveField combinator always produces a non-empty complement. The breaking change detector (panproto-check) will classify the corresponding schema change as breaking (vertex removal).
A RenameField combinator produces an empty complement. The breaking change detector will see the rename as a vertex removal + vertex addition, classifying the removal as breaking. To avoid this, use the migration engine’s rename support instead.
A CoerceType combinator may or may not be breaking depending on the kinds involved. The breaking change detector classifies all kind changes as breaking, which aligns with the conservative interpretation.

This interplay means you can use panproto-check to validate whether a lens is necessary: if the compatibility report says “fully compatible,” a lens is unnecessary (a migration suffices). If it says “breaking,” a lens with complement tracking is the appropriate tool.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.12 Error Handling
The panproto-lens error types cover failures in both the combinator and primitive layers:

LensError::Restrict: the underlying wtype_restrict call failed during get. This typically indicates a mismatch between the lens’s schemas and the instance.
LensError::ComplementMismatch: during put, the complement is inconsistent with the view. This can happen if the complement was generated by a different lens or a different source instance.
LensError::FieldNotFound: a combinator references a field name not present in the schema.
LensError::VertexNotFound: a combinator references a vertex ID not present in the schema.
LensError::IncompatibleCoercion: a CoerceType combinator references a kind not present in any vertex.

For a hands-on introduction to building and applying lenses, see tutorial chapter 8.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.13 Summary
The panproto-lens crate provides three layers of abstraction:

Primitives: get and put operate on Lens values, producing views and complements.
Combinators: RenameField, AddField, RemoveField, WrapInObject, HoistField, CoerceType, and Compose describe transformations declaratively.
Laws: check_laws property-tests GetPut and PutGet to verify correctness.

Together, these layers enable safe, reversible schema evolution across all panproto-supported protocols, even when the evolution involves lossy structural changes. The complement is the key innovation: it makes non-bijective transformations reversible by explicitly tracking what was lost, rather than silently discarding it.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.14 Module Map
The crate is organized into the following modules:

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
section: 10.15 When to Use Lenses vs. Migrations
text:
10.15 When to Use Lenses vs. Migrations
The choice between lenses and migrations depends on the nature of the schema change:








Scenario
Use Migration
Use Lens




Rename a field
Yes (bijective)
Yes (but unnecessary overhead)


Reorder fields
Yes (bijective)
Yes (but unnecessary overhead)


Remove a field
No (not invertible)
Yes (complement stores removed data)


Add a required field
Possible (with default)
Yes (complement stores default)


Change a field’s type
No (not invertible)
Yes (if coercion is defined)


Wrap fields in a new object
No (not invertible)
Yes (complement tracks re-parenting)


Multi-step evolution
Compose migrations
Chain combinators



As a rule of thumb: if panproto-mig’s invert would succeed on your migration, use a migration. If it would fail, use a lens.

title: 10  panproto-lens — Bidirectional Lenses
text:
10.16 Performance Considerations
Lens operations are slightly more expensive than plain migrations because of the complement computation:

get performs one wtype_restrict call (same cost as lift_wtype) plus a set-difference pass over the source instance to compute the complement. The overhead is O(|source nodes|).
put performs a reconstruction pass that is O(|view nodes| + |complement nodes|), with hash-map lookups for un-remapping and contraction choice disambiguation.
Complement serialization depends on the complement size, which is proportional to the amount of data dropped during get.

For lossless transformations (empty complement), get and put have essentially the same cost as a migration lift and its inverse.
In batch scenarios where the same lens is applied to many instances (e.g., projecting a million records to an API view), the lens should be constructed once and reused. The Lens struct is Clone and can be shared across threads via Arc<Lens>. The compiled migration inside the lens is immutable after construction, so concurrent get calls are safe without synchronization.
The complement size is the primary cost driver. A lens that drops a single leaf field produces a small complement (one node, one arc). A lens that removes an entire subtree produces a complement proportional to the subtree size. When designing lens-based APIs, consider whether the complement can be compressed or whether a more targeted lens (removing only the specific fields needed) would reduce overhead.






Clarke, Bryce. 2020. “Internal Lenses as Functors and Cofunctors.” Applied Category Theory 2019, Electronic proceedings in theoretical computer science, vol. 323: 183–95. https://doi.org/10.4204/EPTCS.323.13.


Foster, J. Nathan, Michael B. Greenwald, Jonathan T. Moore, Benjamin C. Pierce, and Alan Schmitt. 2007. “Combinators for Bidirectional Tree Transformations: A Linguistic Approach to the View-Update Problem.” ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems 29 (3): Article 17. https://doi.org/10.1145/1232420.1232424.


Hofmann, Martin, Benjamin C. Pierce, and Daniel Wagner. 2011. “Symmetric Lenses.” Proceedings of the 38th ACM SIGPLAN-SIGACT Symposium on Principles of Programming Languages (POPL 2011), 371–84. https://doi.org/10.1145/1926385.1926428.


Johnson, Michael, and Robert Rosebrugh. 2013. “Delta Lenses and Opfibrations.” Proceedings of the 2nd International Workshop on Bidirectional Transformations (BX 2013), Electronic communications of the EASST, vol. 57. https://doi.org/10.14279/tuj.eceasst.57.875.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.1 Why Breaking Change Detection Matters
The panproto-check crate analyzes schema evolution to determine whether a proposed change is backward-compatible. It produces detailed diagnostics when breaking changes are found, enabling CI pipelines and developer tooling to catch incompatible migrations before they reach production.
This chapter covers the three-step detection pipeline: structural diffing, protocol-aware classification, and human/machine-readable reporting.
The crate lives at crates/panproto-check and is published as panproto-check on crates.io. It depends on panproto-schema for schema and protocol types, rustc-hash for fast set operations, and serde_json for JSON report output.
Schema evolution is routine in long-lived systems. An API adds a field, a database drops a column, a serialization format renames a message. Most of these changes are safe—existing consumers can ignore additions and tolerate relaxed constraints. But some changes are dangerous: removing a field that downstream consumers depend on, tightening a constraint that existing data violates, or changing a field’s type in an incompatible way.
panproto’s breaking change detection automates the process of distinguishing safe from dangerous changes. It integrates into CI pipelines via the schema check CLI command, and into programmatic workflows via the Rust and TypeScript APIs.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.2 The Detection Pipeline
Breaking change detection follows a clean three-stage pipeline:





flowchart LR
    O[Old Schema] --> D[diff]
    N[New Schema] --> D
    D --> SD[SchemaDiff]
    SD --> CL[classify]
    P[Protocol] --> CL
    CL --> CR[CompatReport]
    CR --> RT[report_text]
    CR --> RJ[report_json]






Each stage has a single responsibility:

diff() compares two schemas structurally, recording every added, removed, or modified element. This stage is protocol-agnostic.
classify() interprets the diff through the lens of a specific protocol’s rules, separating breaking from non-breaking changes. This stage is protocol-aware.
report_text() / report_json() renders the classification into a human-readable summary or a machine-consumable JSON value. This stage is format-aware.

The separation is intentional: the same SchemaDiff can be classified differently for different protocols, and the same CompatReport can be rendered in multiple formats. Each stage is independently testable.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.4 Protocol-Aware Classification
Classification is where protocol semantics come into play. The classify function takes a SchemaDiff and a Protocol and determines the severity of each change based on the protocol’s edge rules and constraint sorts.

11.4.1 Breaking Changes
The following changes are always or conditionally classified as breaking:








Change
When Breaking
Rationale




Vertex removal
Always
Consumers depending on the vertex will fail


Edge removal
When the edge kind matches a protocol edge rule
Protocol-governed edges are part of the structural contract


Kind change
Always
Existing data may not conform to the new kind


Constraint tightening
When the sort is recognized by the protocol
Existing data may violate the tighter constraint


Constraint addition
When the sort is recognized by the protocol
Existing data may violate the new constraint



Vertex removal is unconditionally breaking because any consumer relying on that vertex—reading a field, following an edge, anchoring a node—will fail. This is the most severe type of change.
Edge removal is protocol-dependent. The protocol’s edge_rules declare which edge kinds are structurally significant. For example, JSON Schema protocols declare "prop" edges as governed; SQL protocols declare "fk" (foreign key) edges. Removing a governed edge is breaking; removing an ungoverned edge (e.g., a metadata annotation) is not.
Kind changes are unconditionally breaking. Changing a vertex from "string" to "integer" means that existing string data cannot be interpreted as integers without an explicit coercion.


11.4.2 Non-Breaking Changes







Change
Rationale




Vertex addition
New consumers can use it; existing consumers ignore it


Edge addition
Additive change; existing consumers are unaffected


Constraint relaxation
Existing data remains valid under looser constraints


Constraint removal
Equivalent to maximally relaxing the constraint



Vertex and edge additions are the bread and butter of backward-compatible evolution. Adding an optional field to a JSON Schema, a new column to a SQL table, or a new message type to a Protobuf definition—all of these are non-breaking because existing consumers simply ignore the additions.
Constraint relaxation is non-breaking because existing data was valid under the tighter constraint and therefore remains valid under the looser one. Raising maxLength from 300 to 3000, for example, does not invalidate any existing strings.


11.4.3 Constraint Direction
For numeric constraints, the classification engine determines direction automatically:

Upper bounds (maxLength, maxSize, maximum, maxGraphemes): a smaller new value is tightening (breaking); a larger new value is relaxing (non-breaking).
Lower bounds (minLength, minimum): a larger new value is tightening (breaking); a smaller new value is relaxing (non-breaking).
Other constraints: any value change is treated as tightening (breaking), since the engine cannot infer the direction for arbitrary constraint sorts without additional metadata.







Tip



If your protocol defines custom constraint sorts with known directionality, you can extend the is_constraint_tightened function in classify.rs to handle them. For now, unrecognized sorts default to “any change is breaking,” which is the conservative choice.




11.4.4 Compatibility Levels
The CompatReport assigns one of three implicit compatibility levels based on its contents:

Fully compatible: the breaking and non_breaking lists are both empty. The schemas are structurally identical.
Backward compatible: the breaking list is empty but non_breaking is not. Existing consumers are safe; new consumers can use the additions. The compatible field is true.
Breaking: the breaking list is non-empty. At least one change is incompatible. The compatible field is false.







Important



Classification is protocol-dependent. The same SchemaDiff can produce different CompatReport results for different protocols. For example, removing an edge with kind "fk" is breaking under the SQL protocol (which has an edge rule for foreign keys) but might be non-breaking under a protocol that does not govern that edge kind. Always pass the correct protocol to classify.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.5 Reporting
The report module converts a CompatReport into output suitable for humans or machines.

11.5.1 Text Reports
report_text produces a plain-text summary suitable for terminal display:

A compatibility verdict: "COMPATIBLE: No breaking changes detected." or "INCOMPATIBLE: Breaking changes detected."
An itemized list of breaking changes (if any), with a count header and numbered entries.
An itemized list of non-breaking changes (if any), with a count header and numbered entries.
If both lists are empty: "No changes detected."

This is what the schema check CLI command displays by default.


11.5.2 JSON Reports
report_json produces a serde_json::Value with three fields:

compatible (bool): whether the migration is backward-compatible.
breaking (array): each entry describes a breaking change with its type and details.
non_breaking (array): each entry describes a non-breaking change with its type and details.

This format is consumed by CI integrations (e.g., GitHub Actions annotations), the TypeScript SDK, and any tooling that needs machine-readable compatibility verdicts.


11.5.3 Choosing a Report Format
Use report_text for developer-facing output (CLI, pull request comments). Use report_json for machine-facing output (CI gates, automated migration generators, monitoring dashboards).

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.6 Relationship to Migrations and Lenses
Breaking change detection is complementary to the migration and lens systems:

Before migration: run diff + classify to determine whether a migration is safe. If the report says “breaking,” the developer knows to provide a migration with resolvers, supply default values, or use a lens with complement tracking.
With lenses: the RemoveField combinator in panproto-lens always produces a non-empty complement. The check crate can confirm that the corresponding schema change is indeed breaking, validating that the lens is necessary and correctly scoped.
In CI: the schema check CLI command runs this pipeline and exits with a non-zero status on breaking changes, enabling automated gatekeeping. Pull requests that introduce breaking changes without an accompanying migration can be flagged automatically.
In documentation: the text report can be included in changelog entries or release notes to communicate the nature of schema changes to downstream consumers.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.7 Theoretical Foundations
The distinction between breaking and non-breaking changes corresponds to the distinction between open and closed graph morphisms in the double-pushout framework (Ehrig et al. 2006). An open morphism (additive change) preserves all existing structure and extends it; a closed morphism (element removal) may violate the dangling condition, leaving “dangling” references to removed elements.
The protocol-dependent classification extends this by relativizing the notion of “structural significance” to the protocol’s edge rules. This follows the approach of institutional model theory, where the notion of satisfaction is parameterized by the logical institution (Lack and Sobociński 2005). In panproto’s case, the “institution” is the protocol’s theory, and “satisfaction” is backward compatibility.
For a hands-on introduction to detecting breaking changes, see tutorial chapter 9.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
section: 11.8 Worked Example: Evaluating an ATProto Schema Change
text:
11.8 Worked Example: Evaluating an ATProto Schema Change
Consider an ATProto lexicon change that:

Adds a new optional field displayName (vertex addition).
Reduces maxGraphemes on the description field from 3000 to 300 (constraint tightening).
Removes the deprecated legacyId field (vertex removal).

Running the pipeline:
diff produces a SchemaDiff with:

added_vertices: ["post.displayName"]
removed_vertices: ["post.legacyId"]
added_edges: the edge from post to post.displayName
removed_edges: the edge from post to post.legacyId
modified_constraints: maxGraphemes on post.description changed from "3000" to "300"
kind_changes: empty (no type changes)

classify with the ATProto protocol produces a CompatReport with:

Breaking changes (3):

RemovedVertex { vertex_id: "post.legacyId" }
RemovedEdge { src: "post", tgt: "post.legacyId", kind: "prop", name: Some("legacyId") } (because "prop" is governed by ATProto’s edge rules)
ConstraintTightened { vertex_id: "post.description", sort: "maxGraphemes", old_value: "3000", new_value: "300" }

Non-breaking changes (2):

AddedVertex { vertex_id: "post.displayName" }
AddedEdge { src: "post", tgt: "post.displayName", kind: "prop", name: Some("displayName") }

compatible: false

report_text output:
INCOMPATIBLE: Breaking changes detected.

Breaking changes (3):
  1. Removed vertex: post.legacyId
  2. Removed edge: post -> post.legacyId (prop, "legacyId")
  3. Constraint tightened: post.description maxGraphemes 3000 -> 300

Non-breaking changes (2):
  1. Added vertex: post.displayName
  2. Added edge: post -> post.displayName (prop, "displayName")
This report tells the developer that they need to either revert the breaking changes or provide a migration/lens that handles the incompatibility.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.9 Integration with CI
The schema check CLI command wraps this pipeline for use in CI:
schema check --old schema-v1.json --new schema-v2.json --protocol atproto
Exit codes:

0: fully compatible or backward compatible.
1: breaking changes detected.
2: error (e.g., malformed schema, unknown protocol).

For GitHub Actions, the JSON report can be parsed to create inline annotations on the pull request, highlighting the exact fields that changed and whether each change is breaking.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.10 Performance Considerations
The entire pipeline is designed to be fast enough for interactive use:

diff is O(|V_old| + |V_new| + |E_old| + |E_new| + |C_old| + |C_new|) where V, E, and C are vertex, edge, and constraint counts. For typical schemas with hundreds of vertices, this completes in microseconds.
classify is O(|diff changes|), iterating once over each change category.
report_text and report_json are O(|report entries|).

The pipeline can be run on every commit in CI without measurable impact on build times.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.11 Error Handling
The panproto-check crate defines a CheckError type for failures that prevent the pipeline from completing:

Schema deserialization failures (malformed input).
Protocol lookup failures (unknown protocol name).

Note that a CompatReport with breaking changes is not an error—it is a successful analysis that found incompatibilities. The error types cover only cases where the analysis itself cannot be performed.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.12 Summary









Stage
Function
Input
Output




Diff
diff
Old schema + new schema
SchemaDiff


Classify
classify
SchemaDiff + Protocol
CompatReport


Report
report_text / report_json
CompatReport
String / JSON



The key design principle is separation of concerns: diffing is protocol-agnostic, classification is protocol-aware, and reporting is format-aware. This layering makes each stage independently testable and allows new protocols to participate in breaking change detection without any changes to the diff or report modules—only the protocol’s edge rules and constraint sorts need to be defined.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.13 Module Map
The crate is organized into the following modules:








Module
Exports
Purpose




diff
SchemaDiff, ConstraintDiff, ConstraintChange, KindChange, diff
Protocol-agnostic structural diffing


classify
CompatReport, BreakingChange, NonBreakingChange, classify

All public types are re-exported from the crate root.

title: 11  panproto-check — Breaking Change Detection
text:
11.14 Testing Strategy
The test suite validates each pipeline stage:

Diff tests verify that specific structural changes (vertex addition/removal, edge addition/removal, constraint modification, kind change) produce the expected SchemaDiff entries. An important invariant is that diffing identical schemas produces an empty diff.
Classification tests verify that breaking and non-breaking changes are correctly assigned based on protocol edge rules and constraint sorts. Tests cover edge cases like removing an edge whose kind is not governed by any edge rule (should be non-breaking) and tightening a constraint whose sort is not recognized by the protocol (should be ignored).
Report tests verify that text and JSON output formats are correctly structured and contain the expected information.







Ehrig, Hartmut, Karsten Ehrig, Ulrike Prange, and Gabriele Taentzer. 2006. Fundamentals of Algebraic Graph Transformation. Monographs in Theoretical Computer Science. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/3-540-31188-2.


Lack, Stephen, and Paweł Sobociński. 2005. “Adhesive and Quasiadhesive Categories.” RAIRO – Theoretical Informatics and Applications 39 (3): 511–45. https://doi.org/10.1051/ita:2005028.

title: 12  panproto-protocols — Protocol Definitions
text:
12.1 Protocol Categories
The panproto-protocols crate is where abstract category theory meets concrete data formats. It defines 60+ protocol specifications across 9 categories, each built by composing reusable building-block theories via colimit. This chapter explains the theory composition pattern, walks through the building blocks, and provides a guide for adding new protocols.
If you are implementing a new protocol or trying to understand why panproto handles your protocol the way it does, this chapter is the primary reference.
panproto organizes protocols into nine categories:
Despite this diversity, every protocol is defined using the same pattern: compose building-block theories via colimit, define edge rules and vertex kinds, and implement a parser/emitter pair. The combinatorial explosion of protocol-specific logic that plagues conventional interop tools is avoided by factoring common structure into shared theories.

title: 12  panproto-protocols — Protocol Definitions
12  panproto-protocols — Protocol Definitions
text:
12.3 The Colimit Composition Pattern
Each protocol is defined by composing building-block theories via colimit—the categorical operation that glues theories together along shared sorts. The shared sorts act as the “meeting point” where two theories agree on what a concept means.
For example, ATProto’s schema theory is the colimit of ThGraph, ThConstraint, and ThMulti, all sharing the Vertex sort:





graph TD
    ThGraph["ThGraph<br/>(Vertex, Edge, src, tgt)"]
    ThConstraint["ThConstraint<br/>(Vertex, Constraint)"]
    ThMulti["ThMulti<br/>(Vertex, Edge, EdgeLabel)"]
    SharedV["Shared: Vertex"]
    SharedVE["Shared: Vertex, Edge"]

    ThGraph -->|colimit over| SharedV
    ThConstraint -->|colimit over| SharedV
    SharedV --> GC["ThGraph + ThConstraint"]
    GC -->|colimit over| SharedVE
    ThMulti -->|colimit over| SharedVE
    SharedVE --> Schema["ATProto Schema Theory"]

    ThWType["ThWType<br/>(Node, Arc, Value)"] --> Instance["ATProto Instance Theory"]






The colimit ensures that the Vertex sort in ThGraph and the Vertex sort in ThConstraint are identified—they refer to the same concept. The resulting schema theory has all the sorts and operations from both components, with shared sorts unified. This is not a mere union; the colimit preserves the equations and operation signatures from both theories, ensuring that the composed theory is well-formed.

12.3.1 Why Colimit?
The colimit is the right categorical construction because it is the universal way to combine theories while identifying shared structure. Alternatives like products or coproducts either identify too much (products force all sorts to be shared) or too little (coproducts share nothing). The colimit lets you precisely control which sorts are identified and which remain distinct.
In practice, this means:

ThGraph and ThConstraint share Vertex but not Edge and Constraint—edges and constraints are distinct concepts that happen to be attached to the same vertex sort.
ThGraph and ThMulti share both Vertex and Edge—multi-edge labels are an extension of the same edge concept.
ThWType and ThMeta share Node—metadata is an extension of the same node concept.

title: 12  panproto-protocols — Protocol Definitions
text:
12.4 Theory Groups
Protocols cluster into six theory groups based on their schema/instance theory pair. Each group has a corresponding register_* helper function that handles the colimit computation:









Group
Schema Theory
Instance Theory
Registration Helper




A
ThGraph + ThConstraint + ThMulti
ThWType
register_constrained_multigraph_wtype


B
ThHypergraph + ThConstraint
ThFunctor
register_hypergraph_functor


C
ThSimpleGraph + ThConstraint
ThFlat
register_simple_graph_flat


D
ThGraph + ThConstraint + ThMulti + ThInterface
ThWType
register_typed_graph_wtype


E
ThGraph + ThConstraint + ThMulti
ThWType + ThMeta
register_multigraph_wtype_meta




12.4.1 Group A: Constrained Multigraph + W-Type
The largest group, covering protocols with tree-shaped data and potentially parallel edges. ATProto, JSON Schema, OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, MongoDB, YAML Schema, TOML Schema, and many more belong here. The schema theory combines directed graphs (for type relationships), constraints (for validation rules like maxLength), and multi-edge support (for fields that share the same type). Instances are W-type trees.


12.4.2 Group B: Hypergraph + Functor
Covers relational and columnar protocols: SQL, Cassandra, DynamoDB, Parquet, Arrow, DataFrame, CSV/Table Schema. The schema theory uses hypergraphs because tables are multi-arity relations (a table “connects” its columns). Instances are set-valued functors—each table maps to a set of rows.


12.4.3 Group C: Simple Graph + Flat
Covers IDL and serialization protocols: Protobuf, Avro, Thrift, Cap’n Proto, FlatBuffers, ASN.1, Bond, HCL, Redis. These protocols have flat field structures (no deep nesting within a single message/record) and enforce field uniqueness, hence the simple graph. Instances are flat key-value structures.


12.4.4 Group D: Typed Graph + W-Type with Interfaces
Covers type systems and API schemas with inheritance: GraphQL, TypeScript, Python, Rust, Java, Go, Swift, Kotlin, C#. Adds the ThInterface theory to Group A, modeling constructs like GraphQL interfaces, TypeScript interfaces, and Java abstract classes.


12.4.5 Group E: Multigraph + W-Type + Metadata
Covers document formats with mixed content: XML/XSD, HTML, CSS, DOCX, ODF, Neo4j. Combines Group A’s schema theory with an instance theory that adds metadata support (discriminators and extra fields) for elements that have both structured children and unstructured attributes.






Tip



When adding a new protocol, first determine which theory group it belongs to. If it fits an existing group, you can reuse the corresponding register_* function and only need to define the protocol’s edge rules, vertex kinds, and parser/emitter.

title: 12  panproto-protocols — Protocol Definitions
text:
12.5 The Protocol Interface
Each protocol module exports a protocol() function that returns a Protocol struct. The Protocol specifies:

name: the protocol identifier (e.g., "atproto", "json_schema", "sql").
schema_theory and instance_theory: names referencing entries in the theory registry, populated by the register_* helpers.
edge_rules: which edge kinds are structurally significant and what source/target vertex kinds they connect. Used by panproto-check to classify breaking changes (Chapter 11).
obj_kinds: vertex kinds that represent composite objects (as opposed to scalar values like "string" or "integer"). Used during schema parsing and by the lens system.
constraint_sorts: which constraint sorts this protocol recognizes (e.g., "maxLength", "pattern", "maxGraphemes"). Used by both the migration engine’s existence checker and the breaking change detector.

The theory names in the Protocol struct reference entries in the theory registry, which is populated by the register_* helpers. This indirection keeps protocol definitions declarative—a protocol does not construct its own theories, it names them.

title: 12  panproto-protocols — Protocol Definitions
text:
12.6 The Two-Level Presentation Architecture
panproto’s two-parameter architecture (schema theory + instance theory) is mirrored by two crates that provide the presentation functors — the operations connecting raw format syntax to abstract algebraic models:

12.6.1 Schema-Level Presentations (panproto-protocols)
Every protocol implements a bidirectional schema-level conversion:

Parser: parse_*(input: &str) -> Result<Schema, ProtocolError> converts a native format specification (.proto file, JSON Schema, SQL DDL, GraphQL SDL) into panproto’s Schema representation — a model of the protocol’s schema theory \(T_P^S\).
Emitter: emit_*(schema: &Schema) -> Result<String, ProtocolError> converts a Schema back into the native format specification.

Examples:

parse_json_schema(json_str) parses a JSON Schema document into a Schema.
emit_protobuf(schema) emits a .proto file from a Schema.
parse_sql(ddl_str) parses SQL DDL statements into a Schema.



12.6.2 Instance-Level Presentations (panproto-io)
The panproto-io crate provides the complementary instance-level conversions for all 76 protocols:

Parser: parse_wtype(protocol, schema, &bytes) -> Result<WInstance, ParseInstanceError> converts raw data (an HTML document, a CoNLL-U file, a Protobuf wire message) into a WInstance or FInstance — a model of the protocol’s instance theory \(T_P^I\).
Emitter: emit_wtype(protocol, schema, &instance) -> Result<Vec<u8>, EmitInstanceError> converts an instance back to raw format bytes.

The choice of WInstance vs FInstance is determined by the protocol’s instance_theory field, not by implementation preference.


12.6.3 The Complete Pipeline
Together, these two levels of presentation complete the functorial data migration pipeline:
raw spec ──protocols parse──→ Schema v1 ──mig compile──→ CompiledMigration
raw data ──io parse──→ Instance v1 ──inst restrict──→ Instance v2 ──io emit──→ raw data
The commutativity guarantee from Spivak 2012 ensures that parse → restrict → emit composes correctly across all 76 protocols. Both levels support round-trip testing: parse(emit(parse(input))) should produce a structurally equivalent result.


12.6.4 Parsing Strategy
Parsers typically follow a two-phase approach:

Syntactic parsing: use a format-specific parser (e.g., serde_json for JSON Schema, tree-sitter for Protobuf) to produce an AST or intermediate representation.
Semantic translation: walk the AST, creating vertices for types and fields, edges for relationships, constraints for validation rules, and hyper-edges for multi-arity relations.



12.6.5 Emitting Strategy
Emitters reverse the process:

Topology traversal: walk the schema graph, identifying root vertices, following edges in topological order.
Format construction: generate the native syntax for each vertex (type declaration, field definition, constraint annotation) and edge (relationship, containment).

title: 12  panproto-protocols — Protocol Definitions
text:
12.7 Adding a New Protocol
To add a new protocol to panproto, follow these five steps:

12.7.1 Step 1: Identify Component Theories
Determine which building-block theories your protocol needs. Most protocols fit one of the five existing groups. Ask yourself:

Does the protocol have directed edges between fields/types? Use ThGraph.
Does the protocol support parallel edges (e.g., multiple fields with the same type)? Add ThMulti.
Does the protocol have constraints (min/max length, required fields, patterns)? Add ThConstraint.
Does the protocol have multi-arity relations (e.g., SQL foreign keys, table definitions)? Use ThHypergraph instead of ThGraph.
Does the protocol support interfaces or inheritance? Add ThInterface.
Does the protocol have flat field structures with no deep nesting? Use ThFlat as the instance theory.
Does the protocol have tree-structured data? Use ThWType.
Does the protocol have mixed content (structured + unstructured)? Add ThMeta to ThWType.



12.7.2 Step 2: Compose via Colimit
If your protocol fits an existing group, call the corresponding register_* function with your theory names:
// In your protocol module's register function:
theories::register_constrained_multigraph_wtype(
    registry,
    "ThMyProtocolSchema",
    "ThMyProtocolInstance",
);
If your protocol needs a novel combination, compose the theories manually using panproto_gat::colimit, specifying shared sorts at each step. Follow the pattern established by the existing register_* functions.


12.7.3 Step 3: Define Edge Rules and Vertex Kinds
Create a protocol() function in your module:
pub fn protocol() -> Protocol {
    Protocol {
        name: "my_protocol".into(),
        schema_theory: "ThMyProtocolSchema".into(),
        instance_theory: "ThMyProtocolInstance".into(),
        edge_rules: vec![/* ... */],
        obj_kinds: vec!["object".into(), "message".into()],
        constraint_sorts: vec!["maxLength".into()],
    }
}


12.7.4 Step 4: Implement Schema Presentation (panproto-protocols)
If your protocol has a schema definition language (e.g., .proto, DDL, SDL), write parse_my_protocol in the panproto-protocols crate to convert native format specifications into a Schema. Write emit_my_protocol to convert back.
Some protocols (like CoNLL-U) have no separate schema language — the schema is implicit in the format specification. These protocols only need Step 4b.


12.7.5 Step 4b: Implement Instance Presentation (panproto-io)
Write an instance codec in panproto-io by implementing the InstanceParser and InstanceEmitter traits. For most protocols, this means choosing the right shared pathway:

JSON data: use JsonCodec::new("my_protocol") — delegates to the SIMD simd-json pathway.
XML data: use XmlCodec::new("my_protocol") — delegates to the quick-xml pull parser.
Tab/delimited data: use TabularCodec::tsv("my_protocol", "table_name") — delegates to the memchr SIMD pathway.
Custom format: implement InstanceParser and InstanceEmitter directly using a dedicated parsing library.

Register the codec in the appropriate category module’s register_all function and add it to the default_registry test.


12.7.6 Step 5: Add Integration Tests
Add tests at both levels:

Schema round-trip (panproto-protocols): parse a native specification, emit it back, verify the result matches.
Instance round-trip (panproto-io): parse native data, emit it back, re-parse, verify structural equality. Use real-world fixture data, not synthetic examples.
Migration integration: verify that parse → restrict → emit produces valid output.
Breaking-change tests: verify classification logic for your protocol’s edge rules.







Warning



When composing theories via colimit, the shared theory must contain exactly the sorts that should be identified. If you share too few sorts, the colimit will create duplicate sorts that should be unified. If you share too many sorts, you may over-constrain the composition. Review the existing register_* functions for examples of correct sharing patterns.




12.7.7 Worked Example: A Hypothetical “TaskML” Protocol
Suppose you are defining a protocol for a task management markup language with:

Tasks have fields (name, description, priority).
Tasks can have subtasks (nesting).
Fields have validation constraints (maxLength on name, enum values for priority).
No parallel edges (each field name is unique within a task).

Step 1: This maps to Group C (simple graph + flat). The simple graph enforces field uniqueness; the flat instance theory models the non-nested field structure. But wait—subtasks imply nesting, which requires W-type trees. So this is actually Group A (constrained multigraph + W-type), minus the multi-edge support since field names are unique. You could use Group A as-is (the multi-edge capability is unused but harmless) or define a custom composition of ThSimpleGraph + ThConstraint with ThWType.
Step 2: Using Group A for simplicity:
theories::register_constrained_multigraph_wtype(
    registry,
    "ThTaskMLSchema",
    "ThTaskMLInstance",
);
Step 3: Define the protocol:
pub fn protocol() -> Protocol {
    Protocol {
        name: "taskml".into(),
        schema_theory: "ThTaskMLSchema".into(),
        instance_theory: "ThTaskMLInstance".into(),
        edge_rules: vec![EdgeRule {
            edge_kind: "field".into(),
            src_kinds: vec!["task".into()],
            tgt_kinds: vec!["string".into(), "enum".into(), "integer".into()],
        }],
        obj_kinds: vec!["task".into()],
        constraint_sorts: vec!["maxLength".into(), "enumValues".into()],
    }
}
Steps 4–5: Implement parse_taskml and emit_taskml, then write round-trip and migration tests.

title: 12  panproto-protocols — Protocol Definitions
text:
12.8 Theoretical Foundations
The colimit composition pattern implements the categorical notion of a theory morphism between generalized algebraic theories (Cartmell 1986). Each building-block theory is a GAT, and the colimit computes the pushout in the category of GATs, identifying sorts along the span defined by the shared theory.
This is not merely an implementation convenience—it is a formal guarantee. The colimit preserves the equations and well-formedness conditions of both component theories. If ThGraph guarantees that every edge has a source and target vertex, and ThConstraint guarantees that every constraint is attached to a vertex, then the colimit theory inherits both guarantees.
The protocol architecture follows the design principles outlined in Lynch et al. (2024), where data interchange protocols are understood as presentations of algebraic theories and protocol interoperability is mediated by theory morphisms. The panproto-protocols and panproto-io crates together implement both levels of the “model functor” from Lynch 2024: schema presentations map format specifications to abstract schema models, and instance presentations map format data to abstract instance models. This two-level factorization mirrors panproto’s two-parameter architecture and ensures that the full pipeline — from raw bytes through migration to target format — composes correctly by construction.
For a hands-on guide to building your own protocol, see tutorial chapter 10.

title: 12  panproto-protocols — Protocol Definitions
text:
12.9 Summary
The protocol system spans two crates:
panproto-protocols (schema-level presentations) provides:

10 building-block theories (ThGraph, ThSimpleGraph, ThHypergraph, ThConstraint, ThMulti, ThInterface, ThWType, ThFunctor, ThFlat, ThMeta) that can be composed via colimit.
6 theory groups (A through F) covering the structural patterns found across 76 protocols.
Schema-level parse/emit: convert native format specifications (.proto, DDL, SDL) to/from Schema.
A uniform protocol interface: every protocol exports protocol(), register_theories(), and for schema-defining protocols, a parser/emitter pair.

panproto-io (instance-level presentations) provides:

76 protocol codecs implementing InstanceParser and InstanceEmitter traits.
4 shared pathways: SIMD JSON (simd-json), zero-copy XML (quick-xml), SIMD HTML (tl), SIMD tabular (memchr) — enabling ~50 protocols to share optimized core parsing infrastructure.
Format-specific codecs for HTML (tl), Markdown (pulldown-cmark), and CoNLL-U (custom memchr-based parser).
A ProtocolRegistry for runtime protocol dispatch by name.

The colimit composition pattern is what makes panproto’s protocol coverage tractable. Rather than implementing migration, lens, and breaking-change logic separately for each protocol, the shared theory infrastructure ensures that all protocols automatically benefit from the same engine. Adding a new protocol is a matter of choosing the right building blocks, writing a schema parser/emitter, and registering an instance codec.






Cartmell, John. 1986. “Generalised Algebraic Theories and Contextual Categories.” Annals of Pure and Applied Logic 32: 209–43. https://doi.org/10.1016/0168-0072(86)90053-9.


Lynch, Owen, Kris Brown, James Fairbanks, and Evan Patterson. 2024. “GATlab: Modeling and Programming with Generalized Algebraic Theories.” arXiv Preprint arXiv:2404.04837, ahead of print. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2404.04837.

title: 13  panproto-wasm — The WASM Boundary
text:
13.1 Design Rationale
The panproto-wasm crate is the thin glue layer between the Rust core and the TypeScript SDK. It exposes exactly ten #[wasm_bindgen] entry points. Every structured value crossing the boundary is serialized as MessagePack bytes – never as a JS object graph. This chapter explains why, and walks through the implementation.
Two design decisions shape the entire WASM boundary:

title: 13  panproto-wasm — The WASM Boundary
text:
Handle-based API. Complex Rust objects (protocols, schemas, compiled migrations) never cross the boundary. Instead, they live in a Rust-side slab allocator, and JS holds opaque u32 handles. This avoids the overhead and fragility of serde-wasm-bindgen, which must recursively convert Rust structs into JS objects on every call.
MessagePack over serde-wasm-bindgen. Structured arguments (builder operations, migration mappings, records) are encoded as MessagePack byte slices with rmp_serde. MessagePack is faster than JSON for encode/decode, produces smaller payloads, and – critically – is deterministic: the same Rust struct always encodes to the same bytes, which simplifies testing and caching.







Tip



The handle + MessagePack design means the WASM boundary has zero JS-heap allocations for data that stays on the Rust side. The only JS objects created are the Uint8Array wrappers around byte slices.

title: 13  panproto-wasm — The WASM Boundary
section: 13.2 The Resource Enum and Slab Allocator

title: 13  panproto-wasm — The WASM Boundary
13  panproto-wasm — The WASM Boundary

13  panproto-wasm — The WASM Boundary
section: 13.6 Data Flow: JS to Rust and Back
text:
13.6 Data Flow: JS to Rust and Back
The following diagram shows the complete lifecycle of a call across the WASM boundary:





sequenceDiagram
    participant JS as TypeScript SDK
    participant Pack as packToWasm (msgpack)
    participant WB as wasm_bindgen
    participant Rust as panproto-wasm
    participant Unpack as unpackFromWasm

    JS->>Pack: structured JS value
    Pack->>WB: Uint8Array
    WB->>Rust: &[u8] (zero-copy view)
    Rust->>Rust: rmp_serde::from_slice → typed Rust struct
    Rust->>Rust: perform operation
    Rust->>Rust: rmp_serde::to_vec → Vec<u8>
    Rust->>WB: Vec<u8>
    WB->>JS: Uint8Array
    JS->>Unpack: Uint8Array
    Unpack->>JS: structured JS value






The key property is that wasm_bindgen passes byte slices as zero-copy views into WASM linear memory. The only copies happen during MessagePack encode (JS side) and decode (Rust side), plus the reverse on the way back.

title: 13  panproto-wasm — The WASM Boundary
text:
13.7 Slab Lifecycle
Handles follow a simple lifecycle. Freed slots are reused on subsequent allocations:





stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Allocated: alloc(resource)
    Allocated --> InUse: with_resource(handle, f)
    InUse --> Allocated: callback returns
    Allocated --> Freed: free(handle)
    Freed --> Allocated: alloc reuses slot
    Freed --> [*]






On the TypeScript side, WasmHandle wraps each raw u32 handle and calls free_handle in its Symbol.dispose implementation. A FinalizationRegistry acts as a safety net: if a WasmHandle is garbage-collected without being disposed, the registry calls free_handle to prevent leaks.

title: 13  panproto-wasm — The WASM Boundary
text:
13.8 Error Handling
Errors are represented as WasmError variants (an internal thiserror enum) and converted to JsError at the boundary via Into. The JS side receives them as standard Error objects. The five error variants are:

DeserializationFailed – MessagePack input could not be decoded
InvalidHandle – the u32 index does not point to a live resource
TypeMismatch – the resource at a handle is the wrong variant (e.g., expected Protocol, got Schema)
SchemaBuildFailed – a SchemaBuilder operation failed validation
MigrationFailed / LiftFailed / PutFailed – runtime operation errors

title: 13  panproto-wasm — The WASM Boundary
text:
13.9 Adding a New Entry Point
To add a new WASM entry point:

Add a #[wasm_bindgen] pub fn to crates/panproto-wasm/src/api.rs
If the function needs a new resource type, add a variant to Resource in slab.rs and a corresponding as_* extractor
Add the TypeScript signature to WasmExports in sdk/typescript/src/types.ts
Wrap the call in a typed method on the appropriate SDK class
Add an integration test in tests/integration/tests/wasm_boundary.rs







Tip



The check_existence entry point demonstrates the pattern for functions that should never throw across the boundary: wrap the inner logic in a fallible helper, and convert errors into a structured report in the outer function.

title: 14  @panproto/core — The TypeScript SDK
text:
14.1 Architecture Overview
The @panproto/core npm package is the primary way most developers interact with panproto. It wraps the WASM module in a fluent, type-safe API with automatic resource management. This chapter covers every public class and function in the SDK.
The SDK is organized into six core classes, each with a focused responsibility:
classDiagram
    class Panproto {
        +init() Panproto$
        +protocol(name) Protocol
        +defineProtocol(spec) Protocol
        +migration(src, tgt) MigrationBuilder
        +checkExistence(src, tgt, builder) ExistenceReport
        +compose(m1, m2) CompiledMigration
        +diff(old, new) DiffReport
        +Symbol.dispose()
    }

    class Protocol {
        +name: string
        +spec: ProtocolSpec
        +schema() SchemaBuilder
        +Symbol.dispose()
    }

    class SchemaBuilder {
        +vertex(id, kind, opts?) SchemaBuilder
        +edge(src, tgt, kind, opts?) SchemaBuilder
        +hyperEdge(id, kind, sig, parent) SchemaBuilder
        +constraint(vertex, sort, value) SchemaBuilder
        +required(vertex, edges) SchemaBuilder
        +build() BuiltSchema
    }

    class BuiltSchema {
        +data: SchemaData
        +protocol: string
        +vertices: Record
        +edges: Edge[]
        +Symbol.dispose()
    }

    class MigrationBuilder {
        +map(src, tgt) MigrationBuilder
        +mapEdge(src, tgt) MigrationBuilder
        +resolve(src, tgt, edge) MigrationBuilder
        +compile() CompiledMigration
    }

    class CompiledMigration {
        +spec: MigrationSpec
        +lift(record) LiftResult
        +get(record) GetResult
        +put(view, complement) LiftResult
        +Symbol.dispose()
    }

    Panproto --> Protocol : protocol()
    Protocol --> SchemaBuilder : schema()
    SchemaBuilder --> BuiltSchema : build()
    Panproto --> MigrationBuilder : migration()
    MigrationBuilder --> CompiledMigration : compile()
